docs.title: Algorithms
docs.text:
This document explains the core algorithms used in the A-Maze-ing project.

The project mainly relies on: - Depth-First Search (DFS) for maze generation - Breadth-First Search (BFS) for maze solving

These algorithms are responsible for: - creating valid mazes - generating paths - solving the maze - visualizing exploration and traversal

docs.title: 📁 Main Files
docs.text:
mazegen/
├── generator.py
└── solver.py

docs.title: 🧠 Overview
docs.text:
The project uses two different graph traversal algorithms:

Algorithm Purpose DFS Maze generation BFS Shortest path solving

Both algorithms operate on the maze's expanded grid representation.

docs.title: 🌱 DFS Maze Generation
docs.text:
Main function:

def dfs_generator(maze: Maze) -> None:

Main file:

mazegen/generator.py

docs.title: 🧩 Goal of DFS
docs.text:
DFS is used to: - carve paths through walls - visit every logical cell - create a valid maze structure - generate a perfect maze

A perfect maze means: - every cell is reachable - there is only one valid path between cells - no loops exist

docs.title: 🧠 Expanded Grid System
docs.text:
The maze internally uses an expanded grid.

Logical cells are separated by walls.

Example:

# # # # #
# . # . #
# # # # #
# . # . #
# # # # #

Logical coordinates are converted using:

(y * 2 + 1), (x * 2 + 1)

This allows the algorithm to: - carve walls - connect cells - preserve maze structure

docs.title: 🔄 DFS Traversal Logic
docs.text:
The DFS algorithm:

  1. Starts at the first logical cell
  2. Marks the current cell as visited
  3. Randomly selects a direction
  4. Moves two cells away
  5. Breaks the wall between cells
  6. Recursively continues traversal

docs.title: 🎲 Randomized Directions
docs.text:
The directions are shuffled using:

random.sample(directions, len(directions))

This guarantees: - randomized mazes - different layouts every execution - procedural generation behavior

docs.title: 🪓 Wall Breaking
docs.text:
When DFS moves to a new cell:

Current Cell -> Wall -> Next Cell

the wall between both cells is removed.

This creates: - corridors - connected paths - valid maze routes

docs.title: 📌 DFS Characteristics
docs.text: Property Behavior Traversal Type Recursive Path Type Deep exploration Maze Style Long corridors Randomness High Guarantees Fully connected maze

docs.title: 🚪 Entry and Exit Integration
docs.text:
After DFS generation:

apply_entry_exit(maze)

opens: - the entry cell - the exit cell - external maze doors

The project validates that: - entry and exit are located on maze borders - coordinates are valid - doors connect correctly with the expanded grid

docs.title: 🛡️ Maze Validation
docs.text:
The generation system validates: - invalid open spaces - malformed structures - impossible layouts

Main validation:

check_open_areas()

This prevents: - large empty spaces - invalid 3x3 open blocks

docs.title: 🧱 Imperfect Maze Generation
docs.text:
If the maze is configured as:

PERFECT=False

the algorithm uses:

break_walls()

to: - remove extra walls - create loops - generate multiple possible paths

This transforms the perfect DFS maze into an imperfect maze.

docs.title: 🔎 BFS Maze Solving
docs.text:
Main function:

def bfs_solve_maze(maze: Maze)

Main file:

mazegen/solver.py

docs.title: 🎯 Goal of BFS
docs.text: BFS is used to: - find the shortest path - solve the maze - reconstruct the optimal route - generate exploration animations

docs.title: 📦 BFS Data Structures
docs.text:
The solver uses:

Structure Purpose Queue (deque) Exploration order Set (visited) Prevent revisits Dictionary (parent) Path reconstruction List (explored) Animation visualization

docs.title: 🌊 BFS Traversal Logic
docs.text:
The BFS algorithm:

  1. Starts at the entry point
  2. Explores neighboring cells
  3. Expands layer by layer
  4. Continues until the exit is found
  5. Reconstructs the shortest path

Unlike DFS: - BFS explores breadth-first - guarantees the shortest path

docs.title: 🧭 Valid Movement Rules
docs.text:
The solver ignores: - walls (1) - logo cells (2) - already visited cells - out-of-bounds positions

This ensures: - safe traversal - valid pathfinding - no infinite loops

docs.title: 🧩 Path Reconstruction
docs.text:
Once the exit is found:

parent[(new_y, new_x)] = (y, x)

stores: - where each cell came from

The algorithm then reconstructs the path: - backwards from exit - until the entry point is reached

Finally: - the path is reversed - producing the final solution order

docs.title: 🎞️ Explored Cells Animation
docs.text:
BFS also stores:

explored.append((new_y, new_x))

This allows the renderer to: - visualize exploration - animate the solver - display visited cells progressively

docs.title: 📌 BFS Characteristics
docs.text: Property Behavior Traversal Type Iterative Exploration Style Layer-by-layer Path Guarantee Shortest path Memory Usage Higher than DFS Animation Friendly Yes

docs.title: ⚖️ DFS vs BFS
docs.text: DFS BFS Generates mazes Solves mazes Recursive Iterative Deep traversal Breadth traversal Randomized Deterministic Creates paths Finds shortest path

docs.title: 🎯 Algorithm Goals
docs.text:
The algorithm system was designed to: - generate valid procedural mazes - guarantee solvable layouts - provide visual algorithm demonstrations - support animation systems - separate generation and solving logic cleanly

The combination of DFS and BFS creates: - randomized maze structures - reliable shortest-path solving - interactive visualization systems - reproducible procedural generation

docs.title: Export System
docs.text:
This document explains the export and utility systems used in the A-Maze-ing project.

The export system is responsible for: - Converting maze data into hexadecimal values - Exporting maze information into a .txt file - Converting paths into N, E, S, W directions - Handling logical maze cell serialization

docs.title: 📁 Main Files
docs.text:
utils/
├── export_utils.py
├── hex_utils.py
└── path_utils.py

docs.title: 🧠 Overview
docs.text:
The export system converts the generated maze into a structured output format.

The exported file contains: - Hexadecimal maze representation - Entry coordinates - Exit coordinates - BFS solution path directions

This output format follows the project requirements and allows the maze data to be reconstructed later.

docs.title: 📦 Export Pipeline
docs.text:
Generated Maze
      │
      ▼
maze_to_hex()
      │
      ▼
path_to_directions()
      │
      ▼
export_maze()
      │
      ▼
output_maze.txt

docs.title: 📝 Main Export Function
docs.text:
Main file:

utils/export_utils.py

Main function:

def export_maze(
    maze: Maze,
    path: list[tuple[int, int]],
    output_file: str
) -> None:

This function: - converts the maze into hexadecimal values - extracts entry and exit coordinates - converts the BFS path into directions - writes all information into the output file

docs.title: 📄 Exported File Structure
docs.text: The generated output file contains:

docs.title: 1. Hexadecimal Maze Representation
docs.text:
Each logical maze cell is converted into a hexadecimal value.

Example:

F F F F
9 2 A 6
D 4 1 3

Each hexadecimal value represents which walls are closed around the cell.

docs.title: 2. Entry Coordinates
docs.text:
Example:

0,0

docs.title: 3. Exit Coordinates
docs.text:
Example:

19,19

docs.title: 4. Path Directions
docs.text:
Example:

EESSWWNN

The directions represent the shortest BFS path from entry to exit.

docs.title: 🔢 Hexadecimal Conversion
docs.text:
Main file:

utils/hex_utils.py

Main function:

def maze_to_hex(maze: Maze) -> list[list[str]]:

docs.title: 🧠 How the Hex System Works
docs.text:
Each logical maze cell checks its surrounding walls:

Direction Bit Value North 1 East 2 South 4 West 8

The values are summed and converted into hexadecimal.

docs.title: 📌 Example
docs.text:
If a cell has: - North wall - West wall

then:

1 + 8 = 9

Hex value:

9

docs.title: 🧩 Expanded Grid Conversion
docs.text:
The maze internally uses an expanded grid system.

Logical coordinates are converted into grid coordinates:

(y * 2 + 1), (x * 2 + 1)

This allows: - walls - paths - openings

to exist between logical cells.

docs.title: 🧭 Path Direction Conversion
docs.text:
Main file:

utils/path_utils.py

Main function:

def path_to_directions(
    path: list[tuple[int, int]]
) -> list[str]:

This function converts BFS coordinates into: - N - E - S - W

directions.

docs.title: 📌 Example
docs.text:
Coordinate path:

[(1,1), (1,2), (2,2)]

becomes:

["E", "S"]

docs.title: 🖨️ File Printing Utility
docs.text:
The export system also contains a helper function:

def print_maze_file(output_file: str) -> None:

This function: - opens the exported file - reads its contents - prints the result to the terminal

It is mainly used for: - debugging - testing - output verification

docs.title: 🚨 Error Handling
docs.text:
The export system handles: - missing files - permission errors - invalid output access

using try/except blocks.

Example:

print_maze_file() failed -> File not found

docs.title: 🎯 Goals of the Export System
docs.text:
The export system was designed to: - serialize maze data - match project output requirements - simplify debugging - convert internal structures into readable formats - provide reusable utility functions

It acts as the final conversion layer between: - maze generation - pathfinding - file output - external representation

docs.title: Maze Generator
docs.text:
This document explains the reusable maze generation module used in the A-Maze-ing project.

The maze generator is the core system responsible for: - creating the maze structure - applying entry and exit points - validating maze rules - solving the maze - exporting the final result - providing a reusable package through .whl

docs.title: 📁 Main Files
docs.text:
mazegen/
├── __init__.py
├── Maze.py
├── MazeGenerator.py
├── generator.py
└── solver.py

docs.title: 🧱 Maze Class
docs.text:
Main file:

mazegen/Maze.py

The Maze class stores the basic maze data.

class Maze:

It contains: - maze width - maze height - entry coordinates - exit coordinates - expanded grid - expanded grid width - expanded grid height

docs.title: 🧩 Expanded Grid
docs.text:
The project uses an expanded grid representation.

For a maze with logical size:

WIDTH x HEIGHT

the internal grid size becomes:

(2 * WIDTH + 1) x (2 * HEIGHT + 1)

This allows the maze to store: - logical cells - walls between cells - external borders - opened doors

Example:

logical cell -> expanded position

(x, y) -> (x * 2 + 1, y * 2 + 1)

docs.title: 🏗️ MazeGenerator Class
docs.text:
Main file:

mazegen/MazeGenerator.py

The MazeGenerator class controls the full maze generation pipeline.

class MazeGenerator:

It receives:

width: int
height: int
entry: tuple[int, int]
exit: tuple[int, int]
perfect: bool
seed: int | None

docs.title: ⚙️ Generation Pipeline
docs.text:
The main method is:

def generate_maze(self) -> Maze:

This method coordinates the helper functions from generator.py and solver.py.

Pipeline:

MazeGenerator.generate_maze()
        │
        ▼
Create Maze object
        │
        ▼
dfs_generator()
        │
        ▼
apply_entry_exit()
        │
        ▼
check_open_areas()
        │
        ▼
add_42_logo()
        │
        ▼
break_walls() if PERFECT=False
        │
        ▼
bfs_solve_maze()
        │
        ▼
Return valid Maze

docs.title: 🌱 DFS Integration
docs.text:
The generator calls:

dfs_generator(maze)

from:

mazegen/generator.py

This function: - carves the maze paths - visits reachable cells - creates the base perfect maze structure

The DFS details are documented in:

algorithms.md

docs.title: 🚪 Entry and Exit
docs.text:
The generator calls:

apply_entry_exit(maze)

This function: - converts logical entry and exit coordinates into expanded grid coordinates - opens the internal entry and exit cells - validates that entry and exit are placed on maze borders - opens the external door for each one

docs.title: 🛡️ Maze Validation
docs.text:
The generator calls:

check_open_areas(maze)

This function prevents invalid open spaces by rejecting mazes containing a fully open 3x3 area.

If the generated maze does not pass validation, the generator tries again.

docs.title: 4️⃣ 42 Logo Integration
docs.text:
The generator calls:

add_42_logo(maze)

This function adds the visible 42 pattern to the maze.

The logo is represented inside the expanded grid using closed cells.

If the maze is too small for the logo, the function returns an empty list and the program prints a warning message.

docs.title: 🧱 Perfect and Imperfect Mazes
docs.text: The project supports two maze types through the PERFECT configuration value.

docs.title: PERFECT=True
docs.text:
The maze keeps the original DFS-generated structure.

This means: - the maze is fully connected - the maze has a single main route structure - no extra walls are removed after generation

docs.title: PERFECT=False
docs.text:
The generator calls:

break_walls(maze, logo_pos)

This function: - removes additional walls - creates extra routes - makes the maze imperfect - protects the 42 logo cells from being overwritten

docs.title: 🔎 BFS Integration
docs.text:
After generation, the maze is solved using:

bfs_solve_maze(maze)

from:

mazegen/solver.py

This confirms that: - the maze is solvable - a valid path exists between entry and exit

If BFS cannot find a path, the generation attempt is rejected and the generator tries again.

docs.title: 🧭 Solver Method
docs.text:
The class also provides:

def solve(self, algorithm: str) -> tuple[list[tuple[int, int]], list[tuple[int, int]]]:

Currently supported algorithm:

bfs

The method returns: - the final shortest path - explored cells used for pathfinding animation

Example:

path, explored = generator.solve("bfs")

docs.title: 📤 Export Method
docs.text:
The class provides:

def export(self, output_file: str) -> None:

This method calls the export system:

export_maze(self.maze, self.path, output_file)

The export logic is documented in:

export-system.md

docs.title: 📦 Reusable Package
docs.text:
The mazegen directory is designed to work as a reusable Python package.

This allows the maze generation logic to be used outside the main graphical application.

The reusable package contains: - Maze - MazeGenerator - DFS generation helpers - BFS solving helpers

The MLX rendering, menu system, assets, and gameplay logic are not part of the reusable package.

docs.title: 📄 Package Public API
docs.text:
The package exposes its main classes through:

mazegen/__init__.py

Example:

from .MazeGenerator import MazeGenerator
from .Maze import Maze

__all__ = [
    "MazeGenerator",
    "Maze"
]

This allows external projects to import:

from mazegen import MazeGenerator

docs.title: 🛠️ Building the .whl
docs.text:
The package can be built using Python build tools.

Install build:

pip install build

Build the package:

python -m build

This creates:

dist/
├── mazegen-1.0.0.tar.gz
└── mazegen-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: 📥 Installing the Package
docs.text:
The generated wheel can be installed with:

pip install dist/mazegen-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

or force reinstalled with:

pip install --force-reinstall dist/mazegen-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: 🧪 Example Usage
docs.text:
After installing the wheel:

from mazegen import MazeGenerator

generator = MazeGenerator(
    width=20,
    height=20,
    entry=(0, 0),
    exit=(19, 19),
    perfect=True,
    seed=42
)

maze = generator.generate_maze()
path, explored = generator.solve("bfs")

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose of the Maze Generator Module
docs.text:
The maze generator module was designed to: - keep the maze logic separate from rendering - make the project easier to maintain - support reuse in future projects - provide a clean API - satisfy the reusable module requirement - allow package distribution through .whl

This separation makes the project more modular by keeping the core maze engine independent from the visual MLX application.

docs.title: MLX Library
docs.text:
This document explains how the MiniLibX (MLX) library is used in the A-Maze-ing project.

MiniLibX is a small graphical library commonly used in 42 School projects. It provides basic tools to: - open a window - load images - draw images to the screen - handle keyboard input - listen to window events - run a graphical loop

In this project, MLX is used to display the maze, menus, themes, player movement, BFS animations, and visual feedback.

docs.title: 📁 Main Files
docs.text:
render/
├── mlx_renderer.py
├── draw_maze.py
├── menu.py
├── Assets.py
├── converter.py
└── GameState.py

docs.title: 🧠 What MLX Does
docs.text:
MLX works as the graphical layer of the project.

It does not generate the maze and it does not solve the maze.

Instead, it receives data from the project and displays it visually.

Maze generation
      │
      ▼
Maze grid / path / state
      │
      ▼
MLX renderer
      │
      ▼
Window display

docs.title: 🪟 Creating a Window
docs.text:
The first step in any MLX program is initializing MLX and creating a window.

In this project, this happens inside:

def mlx_window(game: GameState) -> None:

Example from the project:

mlx = Mlx()
mlx_ptr = mlx.mlx_init()

win_ptr = mlx.mlx_new_window(
    mlx_ptr,
    WINDOW_WIDTH,
    WINDOW_HEIGHT,
    "A-MAZE-ING"
)

This creates the main game window.

The project uses:

WINDOW_WIDTH = 1600
WINDOW_HEIGHT = 900

docs.title: 🖼️ Images in MLX
docs.text:
MiniLibX works best with .xpm images.

Because of that, the project converts image assets into XPM before loading them.

Examples of loaded assets: - walls - floors - trails - ducks - exit doors - banners - menu backgrounds

docs.title: 📦 Asset Loading
docs.text:
The Assets class loads and stores the images used by the renderer.

Main file:

render/Assets.py

Example:

self.wall, _, _ = mlx.mlx_xpm_file_to_image(
    mlx_ptr,
    f"assets/generated/{wall_file}_{tile_size}.xpm"
)

This loads an XPM image and stores it so it can be drawn later.

docs.title: 🔄 PNG / JPEG to XPM Conversion
docs.text:
The project stores original assets as PNG or JPEG files.

Before MLX can use them, they are: 1. resized 2. converted to XPM 3. saved in assets/generated/

Main file:

render/converter.py

Main functions:

generate_scaled_asset()
convert_to_xpm()
generate_all_assets()

Example:

os.system(f"magick {png_path} {xpm_path}")

The project uses ImageMagick through the magick command to perform the conversion.

docs.title: 📐 Dynamic Asset Scaling
docs.text:
The maze can have different sizes, so the tile size is calculated dynamically.

This prevents the maze from being too large for the screen.

Example:

def calculate_tile_size(game: GameState) -> int:

The tile size depends on: - maze width - maze height - available window width - available window height - banner space

Once the tile size is known, assets are generated for that exact size.

docs.title: 🧱 Drawing Images to the Window
docs.text:
MLX draws images using:

mlx.mlx_put_image_to_window()

Example from the project:

mlx.mlx_put_image_to_window(
    mlx_ptr,
    win_ptr,
    base_tile,
    screen_x,
    screen_y
)

This places an image at a specific pixel position.

docs.title: 🧩 Tile-Based Rendering
docs.text:
The maze is drawn tile by tile.

Each value in the maze grid represents a different visual element:

0 = floor
1 = wall
2 = 42 wall
3 = duck/player
4 = trail/path

The renderer reads the grid and chooses which image to draw.

Example logic:

if cell == 3:
    base_tile = assets.floor
elif cell == 0:
    base_tile = assets.floor
elif cell == 1:
    base_tile = assets.wall
elif cell == 2:
    base_tile = assets.wall_42
else:
    base_tile = assets.trail

docs.title: 🦆 Drawing the Player
docs.text:
The duck is drawn as a separate image on top of the floor.

This is important because the player should not permanently modify the maze grid.

Example:

if cell == 3:
    mlx.mlx_put_image_to_window(
        mlx_ptr,
        win_ptr,
        assets.duck,
        screen_x,
        screen_y
    )

This allows the player to move without destroying walls, floors, or trails.

docs.title: 🚪 Drawing Entry and Exit
docs.text:
The maze entry and exit are logical cells.

For rendering, they are converted to expanded grid positions.

The renderer uses:

logical_to_grid()
calculate_door_position()

These functions are used to draw the entry and exit doors in the correct position.

docs.title: 🧭 Coordinate Systems
docs.text: The project uses two coordinate systems:

docs.title: Logical Coordinates
docs.text:
Used by the configuration file and maze structure.

Example:

ENTRY=0,0
EXIT=19,19

docs.title: Grid Coordinates
docs.text:
Used by the expanded internal maze grid.

Conversion:

grid_x = logical_x * 2 + 1
grid_y = logical_y * 2 + 1

This allows the renderer to draw: - cells - walls - corridors - doors

correctly.

docs.title: 🎮 Keyboard Input
docs.text:
MLX can react to keyboard input using hooks.

In this project, keyboard input is handled with:

mlx.mlx_key_hook()

Example:

mlx.mlx_key_hook(
    win_ptr,
    lambda key, param: key_hook(
        key,
        game,
        frame,
        param,
        mlx,
        mlx_ptr,
        win_ptr
    ),
    None
)

The key_hook() function controls: - menu selection - maze regeneration - BFS animation - player mode - theme switching - exiting the game

docs.title: ⌨️ Key Controls
docs.text:
The project uses numeric keys and arrow keys.

1        Generate new maze
2        Show shortest path
3        Show BFS exploration animation
4        Player mode
5        Change theme
6 / ESC  Exit
Arrows   Move player

docs.title: 🎮 Game State and MLX
docs.text:
MLX itself does not know what mode the game is in.

The project stores that information inside:

class GameState:

The renderer checks the game state every frame.

Example states:

game.mode
game.theme
game.show_path
game.animate_bfs
game.playing
game.game_won

This allows MLX to draw different things depending on the current mode.

docs.title: 🔁 The MLX Loop
docs.text:
MLX uses a loop to keep the window alive.

In this project:

mlx.mlx_loop_hook(
    mlx_ptr,
    on_loop,
    None
)

mlx.mlx_loop(mlx_ptr)

The loop repeatedly calls on_loop().

This function updates: - menu rendering - maze rendering - animations - player movement display - exit animation

docs.title: 🎞️ Animation With MLX
docs.text:
MLX does not provide a complex animation system by itself.

Instead, the project creates animation manually using: - frame counters - time checks - partial path drawing - repeated redraws

Example:

frame = [0]
last_time = [time.time()]

The renderer only advances the animation when enough time has passed.

Example:

if now - last_time[0] >= 0.05:

This prevents animations from running too fast.

docs.title: 🦆 Path Animation
docs.text:
Option 2 shows the shortest path.

The renderer progressively draws part of the path:

game.path[:frame[0]]

This creates the effect of the duck moving through the maze.

docs.title: 🌊 BFS Exploration Animation
docs.text:
Option 3 shows how BFS explores the maze.

Instead of displaying only the final path, it displays explored cells:

game.explored[:frame[0]]

This visualizes the search process step by step.

docs.title: 🚪 Exit Animation
docs.text:
The exit uses two images:

assets.exit
assets.exit2

The renderer alternates between them using time:

animation_frame = int(time.time() * 3.33) % 2

This creates a simple animated portal effect.

docs.title: 🧹 Clearing and Syncing the Window
docs.text:
Sometimes the renderer needs to clear the old screen before drawing a new state.

Example:

mlx.mlx_clear_window(mlx_ptr, win_ptr)

The project also uses:

mlx.mlx_do_sync(mlx_ptr)

to help synchronize visual updates.

This is useful when: - changing themes - regenerating the maze - switching modes - entering player mode

docs.title: ❌ Closing the Window
docs.text:
The project handles the window close event with:

mlx.mlx_hook(
    win_ptr,
    33,
    0,
    close,
    None
)

The close() function exits the program:

def close(param: Any) -> None:
    os._exit(0)

The user can also quit with:

ESC
6

docs.title: 🎨 Themes With MLX
docs.text:
The renderer supports different visual themes.

Current themes:

normal
gothic

The selected theme changes which assets are loaded.

Example:

f"assets/generated/{theme}_floor_{tile_size}.xpm"

This allows the same maze logic to be displayed with different visual styles.

docs.title: 🧱 MLX Limitations
docs.text:
MLX is intentionally simple.

It does not provide: - advanced UI widgets - built-in animation tools - sprite management - automatic scaling - layout systems - scene management

Because of that, the project manually handles: - asset conversion - image scaling - menu drawing - animation timing - player movement - screen updates

docs.title: 🧠 Why MLX Was Used
docs.text:
MLX was used because: - it is part of the 42 graphics ecosystem - it teaches low-level rendering concepts - it gives direct control over pixels and images - it integrates well with simple game loops - it is suitable for tile-based projects

For this project, MLX is enough to create: - a graphical maze - menus - animations - player movement - theme switching

docs.title: 🏗️ Role of MLX in A-Maze-ing
docs.text:
MLX acts as the visual interface between the user and the maze engine.

MazeGenerator
      │
      ▼
Maze + Path + Explored Cells
      │
      ▼
GameState
      │
      ▼
MLX Renderer
      │
      ▼
Window Output

The maze generation system stays independent from MLX.

This separation keeps the project modular: - mazegen/ handles maze logic - render/ handles graphics and interaction

docs.title: 🎯 Summary
docs.text:
In A-Maze-ing, MiniLibX is used to: - open the game window - load XPM images - draw the maze - render menus and banners - handle keyboard input - animate BFS and pathfinding - display player movement - support visual themes

The project builds a higher-level rendering system on top of MLX by combining: - GameState - dynamic assets - tile rendering - event hooks - animation logic

This allows a simple graphics library to support a complete interactive maze visualizer.

docs.title: Parsing System
docs.text:
This document explains the configuration parsing system used in the A-Maze-ing project.

The parser is responsible for: - Reading maze configuration files - Validating required parameters - Converting values to proper Python types - Detecting invalid or duplicated data - Ensuring the maze configuration is valid before generation

docs.title: 📁 Main File
docs.text:
parsing/
└── config_parser.py

docs.title: 🧠 Overview
docs.text:
The parser uses a simple KEY=VALUE configuration format.

Example:

WIDTH=20
HEIGHT=20
ENTRY=0,0
EXIT=19,19
OUTPUT_FILE=output_maze.txt
PERFECT=True
SEED=42

The configuration is: - read from a .txt file - converted into a Python dictionary - validated - transformed into usable Python types

docs.title: ⚙️ Main Responsibilities
docs.text:
The parsing system performs several validation and transformation steps:

  • File loading
  • Syntax validation
  • Required key validation
  • Duplicate key detection
  • Type conversion
  • Semantic validation

docs.title: 📥 Configuration File Path
docs.text:
The parser first checks if a configuration file was provided through command-line arguments.

def get_config_path() -> str:

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
• If a .txt file path is provided:
  • it is used as the configuration source
  • If no argument is provided:
  • an exception is raised

docs.title: 🔑 Mandatory Keys Validation
docs.text:
The parser validates that all required configuration keys exist.

Required keys:

WIDTH
HEIGHT
ENTRY
EXIT
OUTPUT_FILE
PERFECT

If one or more keys are missing:

ConfigError:
Missing required key(s)

is raised.

docs.title: 📝 Configuration Parsing
docs.text:
The main parsing function is:

def parse_config(file_path: str) -> dict[str, Any]:

This function: - opens the configuration file - reads it line by line - validates syntax - stores values in a dictionary

docs.title: Empty Lines
docs.text:
Empty lines are ignored.

Example:

WIDTH=20

HEIGHT=20

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
Lines starting with # are ignored.

Example:

# Maze configuration
WIDTH=20

docs.title: Duplicate Key Detection
docs.text:
If the same key appears twice:

WIDTH=20
WIDTH=30

the parser raises:

Duplicate key 'WIDTH'

docs.title: Invalid Format Detection
docs.text:
If a line does not contain =:

WIDTH 20

the parser raises:

Invalid format on line X

docs.title: 🔄 Type Conversion System
docs.text:
After parsing the raw strings, the values are converted into proper Python types.

Main conversion function:

def convert_config(config: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

docs.title: 🔢 Integer Conversion
docs.text:
The following keys are converted to integers:

WIDTH
HEIGHT
SEED

Example:

WIDTH=20

becomes:

20

docs.title: 📍 Coordinate Conversion
docs.text:
The following keys are converted into tuples:

ENTRY
EXIT

Example:

ENTRY=0,0

becomes:

(0, 0)

docs.title: ✅ Boolean Conversion
docs.text:
The PERFECT key is converted into a boolean value.

Example:

PERFECT=True

becomes:

True

Supported values:

True
False

Invalid values raise:

PERFECT must be True or False

docs.title: 🛡️ Semantic Validation
docs.text: The parser also validates logical maze constraints.

docs.title: Positive Dimensions
docs.text:
Maze dimensions must be positive.

Invalid example:

WIDTH=-5

docs.title: Entry and Exit Validation
docs.text:
The parser ensures: - entry and exit are different - coordinates are inside maze bounds

Invalid example:

ENTRY=50,50

for a small maze raises an exception.

docs.title: 🚨 Error Handling
docs.text:
The parsing system uses a custom exception:

class ConfigError(Exception):

This provides: - cleaner error reporting - easier debugging - centralized configuration validation errors

docs.title: 🧩 Parsing Pipeline
docs.text:
config.txt
     │
     ▼
parse_config()
     │
     ▼
Dictionary Creation
     │
     ▼
Mandatory Key Validation
     │
     ▼
convert_config()
     │
     ▼
Type Conversion
     │
     ▼
Semantic Validation
     │
     ▼
Validated Configuration

docs.title: 📦 Final Output Example
docs.text:
After parsing and conversion:

{
    "WIDTH": 20,
    "HEIGHT": 20,
    "ENTRY": (0, 0),
    "EXIT": (19, 19),
    "OUTPUT_FILE": "output_maze.txt",
    "PERFECT": True,
    "SEED": 42
}

docs.title: 🎯 Goals of the Parsing System
docs.text:
The parsing system was designed to: - simplify maze configuration - provide strong validation - prevent invalid maze generation - improve debugging - keep the project modular and maintainable

It acts as the first validation layer before any maze generation or rendering begins.

docs.title: Render System
docs.text:
This document explains the rendering systems used in the A-Maze-ing project.

The render system is responsible for: - graphical visualization using MiniLibX - menu and banner rendering - asset loading and scaling - player visualization - animation systems - real-time game updates

docs.title: 📁 Main Files
docs.text:
render/
├── Assets.py
├── converter.py
├── draw_maze.py
├── GameState.py
├── menu.py
└── mlx_renderer.py

docs.title: 🧠 Overview
docs.text:
The rendering system combines: - MiniLibX graphical rendering - dynamic asset loading - tile-based visualization - gameplay systems - real-time animations - menu and UI rendering

The renderer is responsible for: - drawing the maze - displaying gameplay elements - animating BFS/path systems - handling themes - rendering player movement - updating the graphical interface in real time

docs.title: 🖼️ MLX Rendering System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/mlx_renderer.py

Main function:

def mlx_window(game: GameState) -> None:

This function: - initializes MLX - creates the game window - loads graphical assets - handles events - updates the rendering loop

docs.title: 🪟 Window System
docs.text:
The game window uses:

WINDOW_WIDTH = 1600
WINDOW_HEIGHT = 900

The renderer dynamically scales the maze to fit inside the window.

The bottom section of the screen is reserved for the banner UI.

docs.title: 🎮 Game Modes
docs.text:
The renderer supports multiple game states.

Main states:

Mode Description MENU Theme selection screen GAME Main gameplay screen

Gameplay options:

Key Action 1 Generate new maze 2 Show BFS solution path 3 Show BFS exploration animation 4 Player mode 5 Change theme ESC / 6 Exit game

docs.title: 🧩 GameState System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/GameState.py

The GameState class stores all runtime game information.

class GameState:

It stores: - current mode - selected theme - animation states - player position - current maze - BFS path - explored nodes - gameplay flags

docs.title: 🧠 Main Stored Data
docs.text:
Examples:

self.mode
self.theme
self.playing
self.show_path
self.animate_bfs
self.player_x
self.player_y
self.path
self.explored

The class acts as a centralized runtime state manager.

docs.title: 🎨 Asset System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/Assets.py

The Assets class loads all XPM images used by the game.

class Assets:

Loaded assets include: - walls - floors - trails - ducks - exit doors - animated exits - banners

docs.title: 🖼️ Theme System
docs.text:
The renderer supports multiple themes.

Current themes:

normal
gothic

The selected theme changes: - walls - floors - ducks - trails - exits

The assets are loaded dynamically depending on: - theme - tile size

docs.title: 🔄 Asset Conversion System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/converter.py

The project stores original assets as: - PNG - JPEG

They are dynamically converted into: - XPM

because MiniLibX requires the XPM format.

docs.title: 📦 Asset Generation Pipeline
docs.text:
The conversion system:

  1. loads the original image
  2. resizes the image
  3. converts the image into XPM
  4. stores the generated file

Main functions:

generate_scaled_asset()
convert_to_xpm()
generate_all_assets()

docs.title: 📐 Dynamic Tile Scaling
docs.text:
The renderer dynamically calculates tile size:

def calculate_tile_size(game: GameState) -> int:

This allows: - small mazes - large mazes - fullscreen scaling

without manually resizing assets.

docs.title: 🧱 Maze Drawing System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/draw_maze.py

Main function:

def draw_maze(...)

This function: - renders the maze grid - draws walls and floors - draws the player - draws BFS trails - renders the animated exit - centers the maze on screen

docs.title: 🧩 Coordinate Conversion
docs.text:
The renderer uses helper functions:

logical_to_grid()
grid_to_logical()

These functions convert: - logical maze coordinates - expanded grid coordinates

This keeps rendering synchronized with: - BFS - DFS - gameplay movement

docs.title: 🚪 Door Rendering
docs.text:
The renderer calculates visual entry and exit doors using:

calculate_door_position()

This function: - determines which maze edge the door belongs to - offsets the position correctly - renders the external maze openings

docs.title: 🎞️ Animation System
docs.text: The project supports several animations.

docs.title: 🦆 Path Animation
docs.text:
Option 2 displays: - the shortest BFS path - the duck progressively moving through the maze

The animation progressively reveals:

game.path[:frame]

docs.title: 🌊 BFS Exploration Animation
docs.text:
Option 3 visualizes: - BFS traversal - explored cells - search progression

The animation uses:

game.explored[:frame]

to progressively display explored positions.

docs.title: 🚪 Exit Animation
docs.text:
When: - BFS finishes - the player reaches the exit

the exit portal becomes animated by alternating between:

assets.exit
assets.exit2

using timed frame switching.

docs.title: 🎮 Player Mode
docs.text:
Player mode allows real-time maze traversal using arrow keys.

Main function:

handle_player_movement()

Main features: - collision detection - smooth movement - trail rendering - backtracking - exit detection

docs.title: 🧱 Collision Detection
docs.text:
The player cannot move through: - walls (1) - logo blocks (2)

The movement system validates: - boundaries - blocked cells - valid traversal positions

before updating the player position.

docs.title: 👣 Player Trail System
docs.text:
The renderer stores the player path:

game.player_path

This allows: - visual backtracking - persistent player trails - movement visualization

When the player walks backwards: - the trail is removed dynamically

docs.title: 📜 Menu System
docs.text:
Main file:

render/menu.py

The menu system handles: - menu backgrounds - banner rendering - menu image loading - dynamic image generation

docs.title: 🖼️ Banner Rendering
docs.text:
The bottom banner acts as the game's UI.

It contains: - gameplay options - controls - theme information

The banner is rendered independently from the maze.

docs.title: ⚡ Rendering Loop
docs.text:
The MLX loop uses:

mlx.mlx_loop_hook()

This continuously: - redraws the screen - updates animations - refreshes gameplay state - synchronizes rendering

The project also uses:

mlx.mlx_do_sync()

to improve rendering consistency.

docs.title: 🧠 Rendering Goals
docs.text:
The rendering system was designed to: - provide real-time visualization - separate graphics from algorithms - support gameplay interaction - create animated algorithm demonstrations - dynamically scale assets - keep the project modular

The renderer acts as the visual layer between: - maze generation - gameplay systems - BFS/DFS algorithms - user interaction

docs.title: 📍 Zone System
docs.text:
Every map is composed of connected zones.

Each zone belongs to one of the following types:

Zone Type Description Movement Cost Special Rules 🟢 normal Default zone type 1 turn - Default capacity: 1 drone- Drones may enter, leave and wait 🔴 restricted Sensitive or dangerous zone 2 turns - Drone occupies the connection during transit- Drone MUST arrive next turn- Cannot stop or wait mid-connection- Movement should not start if destination becomes unavailable 🟡 priority Preferred routing zone 1 turn - Should be prioritized during pathfinding- Behaves like a normal zone regarding occupancy ⚫ blocked Inaccessible zone ❌ - Drones cannot enter- Drones cannot cross- Any route using it is invalid

docs.title: 📦 Zone Capacity Rules
docs.text:
Rule Description Default capacity Every zone supports 1 drone unless specified otherwise Custom capacity Zones may define max_drones=N Capacity restriction A drone cannot enter a full zone Simultaneous occupancy Allowed only if capacity permits

Example:

hub: corridorA 4 3 [max_drones=2]

Meaning:

Up to 2 drones may occupy the zone simultaneously.

docs.title: ✅ Special Zones
docs.text: Zone Special Behaviour start_hub Unlimited drones may occupy it initially end_hub Unlimited drones may arrive and delivered drones stop being tracked

docs.title: 🔗 Connection Rules
docs.text:
Connections define possible movement paths.

Example:

connection: A-B

docs.title: ↔️ Bidirectional Connections
docs.text:
All connections are:

  • bidirectional

Meaning:

A-B

allows:

  • A → B
  • B → A

docs.title: 🚫 Invalid Connections
docs.text:
Connections are invalid if:

  • one zone does not exist
  • duplicated connection exists
  • syntax is invalid

The following are duplicates:

A-B
B-A

docs.title: 📦 Connection Capacity
docs.text:
Connections may define:

[max_link_capacity=N]

Example:

connection: A-B [max_link_capacity=2]

Meaning:

Only 2 drones may traverse this connection simultaneously.

docs.title: 🚨 Connection Restrictions
docs.text:
A drone cannot use a connection if:

  • its capacity would be exceeded
  • another movement rule would be violated

docs.title: ⏱️ Turn System
docs.text:
The simulation is turn-based.

At every turn, each drone may:

  • move
  • wait
  • continue restricted-zone transit

docs.title: 🚶 Movement Costs
docs.text: Destination Zone Cost Notes normal 1 turn Immediate movement priority 1 turn Should be preferred during pathfinding restricted 2 turns Drone occupies connection during transit blocked ❌ Cannot be entered

docs.title: 🛫 Restricted Movement Rules
docs.text: Restricted movement behaves differently from normal movement.

docs.title: Turn 1
docs.text:
The drone enters transit state.

Example:

D1-A-B

docs.title: Turn 2
docs.text:
The drone MUST arrive at destination.

The drone cannot:

  • cancel movement
  • wait on the connection
  • pause transit

docs.title: 🔄 Simultaneous Movement Rules
docs.text:
Multiple drones may move during the same turn.

BUT:

  • zone capacities must be respected
  • connection capacities must be respected
  • collisions must not happen

docs.title: 🚨 Occupancy Rules
docs.text:
Two drones cannot:

  • enter the same zone simultaneously

unless:

max_drones > 1

docs.title: 🔓 Freeing Space
docs.text:
When a drone leaves a zone:

  • that space becomes available during the same turn

This means movement scheduling matters.

docs.title: 🧠 Pathfinding Logic Rules
docs.text:
The routing system must consider:

  • shortest paths
  • movement costs
  • restricted zones
  • priority zones
  • occupancy limits
  • connection limits
  • simultaneous drone movement

docs.title: 🚫 Invalid Drone Behaviour
docs.text:
A drone must never:

  • enter blocked zones
  • exceed zone capacity
  • exceed connection capacity
  • remain inside restricted transit longer than allowed
  • teleport
  • skip turns illegally
  • use undefined connections

docs.title: 📄 Output Rules
docs.text:
Every line represents:

1 simulation turn

Format:

D<ID>-<destination>

Example:

D1-roof1 D2-corridorA

docs.title: 🛫 Restricted Transit Output
docs.text:
While travelling toward restricted zones:

D<ID>-<connection>

may be displayed.

Example:

D1-A-B

docs.title: 🏁 End Conditions
docs.text:
Simulation ends only when:

  • every drone reaches end_hub

Delivered drones:

  • are removed from active simulation tracking.

docs.title: 🎯 Optimization Goal
docs.text:
Main objective:

Minimize total simulation turns

The algorithm should:

  • maximize throughput
  • reduce waiting
  • avoid deadlocks
  • distribute drones efficiently across paths

docs.title: 🛩️ Fly-in Input System
docs.text:
The project receives a .txt file describing:

  • the drone count
  • all available zones
  • zone metadata
  • all graph connections
  • movement constraints

The parser is responsible for:

  • reading the file
  • validating syntax
  • validating rules
  • converting raw text into internal objects

docs.title: 📦 Example Input File
docs.text:
# Drone count
nb_drones: 5

# Start and end zones
start_hub: hub 0 0 [color=green]
end_hub: goal 10 10 [color=yellow]

# Regular zones
hub: roof1 3 4 [zone=restricted color=red]
hub: roof2 6 2 [zone=normal color=blue]
hub: corridorA 4 3 [zone=priority color=green max_drones=2]
hub: tunnelB 7 4 [zone=normal color=red]
hub: obstacleX 5 5 [zone=blocked color=gray]

# Connections
connection: hub-roof1
connection: hub-corridorA
connection: roof1-roof2
connection: roof2-goal
connection: corridorA-tunnelB [max_link_capacity=2]
connection: tunnelB-goal

docs.title: 🧾 File Structure
docs.text:
The parser reads the file line by line.

Each line may represent:

  • drone count
  • zone declaration
  • connection declaration
  • comments
  • empty lines

docs.title: 💬 Comments
docs.text:
Lines starting with # are comments.

  • comments are ignored by the parser
  • comments may appear anywhere
  • empty lines should also be ignored

Example:

# This is a comment

docs.title: 🔢 Drone Count
docs.text:
The first mandatory entry defines:

nb_drones: <number>

Example:

nb_drones: 5

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• must exist
  • must be a positive integer
  • only one definition is allowed

docs.title: 📍 Zone Definitions
docs.text: Type Example Rules 🟢 Start Zone start_hub: hub 0 0 - Exactly one start zone must exist- Coordinates are mandatory- Coordinates are always integers 🔴 End Zone end_hub: goal 10 10 - Exactly one end zone must exist- Coordinates are mandatory- Coordinates are always integers 🔵 Regular Zone hub: roof1 3 4 - Zone names must be unique- Coordinates are mandatory- Coordinates are integers

docs.title: 🚫 Invalid Zone Names
docs.text: Invalid Valid my-zone my_zone my zone roof1

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• zone names cannot contain spaces
  • zone names cannot contain dashes (-)

docs.title: 📦 Optional Metadata
docs.text:
Zones may include optional metadata inside:

[ ... ]

Example:

hub: roof1 3 4 [zone=restricted color=red]

Metadata order does not matter.

docs.title: 🏷️ Zone Metadata
docs.text: Metadata Example Rules zone=<type> zone=restricted Allowed values: normal, restricted, priority, blocked color=<value> color=red Optional single-word string max_drones=<number> max_drones=2 Must be a positive integer

docs.title: 🔗 Connection Definitions
docs.text: Connections define graph edges.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
connection: zoneA-zoneB

Example:

connection: roof1-roof2

docs.title: ↔️ Connection Rules
docs.text:
Connections are:

  • bidirectional

Meaning:

A-B

allows:

  • A → B
  • B → A

docs.title: 🚫 Invalid Connections
docs.text:
Connections are invalid if:

  • zones do not exist
  • duplicate edges exist
  • syntax is malformed

Example duplicates:

A-B
B-A

docs.title: 📦 Connection Metadata
docs.text:
Connections may also contain metadata:

[max_link_capacity=N]

Example:

connection: A-B [max_link_capacity=2]

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• optional
  • positive integer only

docs.title: ⚠️ Parsing Validation
docs.text:
The parser should validate:

  • duplicated zones
  • duplicated connections
  • invalid metadata
  • invalid zone types
  • invalid capacities
  • malformed syntax
  • missing start/end zones
  • invalid coordinates

docs.title: 🚨 Error Handling
docs.text:
Any parsing error should:

  • stop parsing immediately
  • return a clear error message

Ideally including:

  • line number
  • reason of failure

Example:

Line 12:
Invalid zone type: lava

docs.title: 🧠 Parsing Goal
docs.text:
The parsing system should transform the raw .txt file into:

  • graph structures
  • zone objects
  • connection objects
  • metadata structures

that will later be used by:

  • pathfinding
  • simulation
  • scheduling
  • visualization systems

docs.title: Dijkstra Algorithm
docs.text: Understanding weighted graph pathfinding and cheapest-route calculation (used on the fly-in project).

docs.title: Table of Contents

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is Dijkstra?
docs.text:
Dijkstra is a graph algorithm used to:

  • find cheapest paths
  • calculate weighted routes
  • optimize movement cost
  • solve weighted graph problems

docs.title: Dijkstra focuses on:
docs.text:
lowest total cost

NOT:

fewest movements

docs.title: 2️⃣ Weighted Graphs
docs.text:
A weighted graph means:

connections or zones have different costs

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
normal      = 1
priority    = 1
restricted  = 2
blocked     = impossible

This means:

not all paths cost the same

docs.title: BFS thinks:
docs.text: fewest steps

docs.title: Dijkstra thinks:
docs.text: lowest total cost

docs.title: Path A
docs.text:
start -> restricted -> goal

Cost:

3 turns

docs.title: Path B
docs.text:
start -> normal -> normal -> goal

Cost:

3 turns

docs.title: Path C
docs.text:
start -> priority -> goal

Cost:

2 turns

Dijkstra chooses:

Path C

because:

lowest total cost

docs.title: 4️⃣ Priority Queue (heapq)
docs.text:
Dijkstra usually uses:

import heapq

because it constantly needs:

cheapest current node

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
queue = []

heapq.heappush(queue, (1, "A"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (5, "B"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (2, "C"))

docs.title: heapq automatically organizes:
docs.text: lowest cost first

docs.title: Then:
docs.text:
heapq.heappop(queue)

returns:

(1, "A")

docs.title: 5️⃣ Cost Tracking
docs.text:
Dijkstra stores:

best known cost for every node

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
costs = {
    "start": 0,
    "A": 1,
    "B": 4,
    "goal": 2
}

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text:
If a cheaper route is discovered:

update cost

docs.title: 6️⃣ Parent Tracking
docs.text:
Dijkstra also stores:

where each node came from

This allows:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
parents = {
    "A": "start",
    "goal": "A"
}

docs.title: 7️⃣ Visited Nodes
docs.text:
Dijkstra tracks visited nodes to avoid:

  • unnecessary processing
  • loops
  • recalculating explored routes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
visited = {
    "start",
    "A"
}

docs.title: 8️⃣ Dijkstra Logic
docs.text:
Dijkstra works by constantly exploring:

The currently cheapest known route

The algorithm starts at the initial node and gradually expands through the graph.

Every time it discovers a cheaper route to a node:

  • the cost is updated
  • the route information is updated
  • the node becomes a better candidate for exploration

docs.title: Cheapest-first exploration
docs.text:
Unlike BFS, Dijkstra does not explore based on:

arrival order

Instead, it explores based on:

lowest total cost

docs.title: Continuous optimization
docs.text:
The algorithm constantly compares:

old path cost
vs
new possible path cost

If the new route is cheaper:

replace old cost

docs.title: Progressive expansion
docs.text:
Dijkstra gradually expands through the graph:

start
 ↓
cheap neighbors
 ↓
slightly more expensive neighbors
 ↓
more expensive neighbors

The graph is explored in order of increasing total cost.

docs.title: Weighted movement
docs.text:
This is why Dijkstra is extremely useful for:

  • traffic systems
  • GPS navigation
  • weighted movement games
  • drone routing
  • path optimization

docs.title: 9️⃣ Path Reconstruction
docs.text:
Once the goal is reached:

walk backwards using parents

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
goal
 ↑
A
 ↑
start

Reverse result:

start -> A -> goal

docs.title: 🔟 Visual Example
docs.text:
Graph:

        start
       /     \
     (1)     (5)
     A         B
      \
      (1)
        \
        goal

docs.title: Exploration
docs.text:
Dijkstra sees:

A = cost 1
B = cost 5

It explores:

A first

because:

1 < 5

docs.title: Final Path
docs.text:
start -> A -> goal

Total cost:

2

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Fly-in Connection
docs.text: Fly-in is essentially:

docs.title: weighted graph pathfinding
docs.text:
because:

  • movement costs exist
  • restricted zones cost more
  • blocked zones are invalid
  • drones need optimized routes

docs.title: Dijkstra fits Fly-in because it:
docs.text:
• minimizes total movement cost
  • avoids blocked zones
  • supports weighted routing
  • generates optimal paths

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Explores:

fewest steps

Uses:

deque

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Explores:

lowest cost path

Uses:

heapq

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Good for:

  • unweighted graphs
  • same movement costs

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Good for:

  • weighted graphs
  • route optimization
  • movement costs

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ Complexity
docs.text:
Typical Dijkstra complexity:

O((V + E) log V)

Where:

V = vertices
E = edges

docs.title: Why more expensive than BFS?
docs.text:
Because:

heapq sorting adds extra work

BUT:

weighted pathfinding requires it

docs.title: 1️⃣4️⃣ Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of Dijkstra like:

GPS route optimization

The algorithm constantly asks:

"What is currently the cheapest route?"

NOT:

"What was explored first?"

docs.title: BFS
docs.text: Closest room first

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text: Cheapest road first

docs.title: Queue vs Heap Queue (heapq)
docs.text: Understanding why BFS uses queues and Dijkstra uses priority queues.

docs.title: What is a Queue?
docs.text:
A queue works like:

First In
First Out

This is called:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
[A, B, C]

Removing:

A

because:

A entered first

docs.title: deque
docs.text:
Python provides:

from collections import deque

deque means:

double-ended queue

Useful operations:

append()
popleft()

docs.title: Why BFS uses deque
docs.text:
BFS explores:

level by level

Example:

start
 ├── A
 ├── B
 └── C

BFS processes:

A first
then B
then C

because:

they were inserted first

docs.title: BFS Behavior
docs.text:
ORDER OF ARRIVAL

This is PERFECT for:

deque

docs.title: What is heapq?
docs.text: heapq is Python's built-in:

docs.title: Difference from normal queue
docs.text:
A normal queue removes:

oldest element

A priority queue removes:

lowest cost first

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
You insert:

(5, "B")
(1, "A")
(3, "C")

docs.title: heapq automatically organizes:
docs.text:
lowest cost first

Then:

heapq.heappop(queue)

returns:

(1, "A")

EVEN IF:

A was inserted later

docs.title: Why Dijkstra uses heapq
docs.text:
Dijkstra constantly asks:

"What is the CHEAPEST current route?"

NOT:

"What was inserted first?"

That is why:

deque

is not ideal for Dijkstra.

docs.title: Dijkstra needs:
docs.text:
LOWEST COST FIRST

Which is exactly what:

heapq

does.

docs.title: Dijkstra Example
docs.text:
import heapq

queue = []

heapq.heappush(queue, (2, "roof1"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (1, "corridorA"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (5, "goal"))

print(heapq.heappop(queue))

Output:

(1, "corridorA")

docs.title: deque / BFS
docs.text:
ORDER OF ARRIVAL

A → B → C

docs.title: heapq / Dijkstra
docs.text:
LOWEST COST FIRST

1 → 2 → 5

docs.title: BFS
docs.text: Good for: - unweighted graphs - same movement costs

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Good for: - weighted graphs - movement costs - route optimization

Fly-in is:

Weighted Graph Pathfinding

So:

docs.title: heapq + Dijkstra
docs.text: fits the project much better.

docs.title: BFS / deque
docs.text:
Think:

"Who arrived first?"

docs.title: Dijkstra / heapq
docs.text:
Think:

"Who is currently the cheapest?"

docs.title: deque
docs.text:
Supermarket line

First customer: - enters first - leaves first

docs.title: heapq
docs.text:
Hospital emergency room

Most urgent patient: - treated first - even if arrived later

docs.text: Understanding how the simulation engine coordinates drones, turns and movement rules in Fly-in.

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is the Simulator?
docs.text:
The Simulator is the component responsible for:

coordinating all drone activity

While other systems may:

  • load map data
  • validate inputs
  • calculate routes

the Simulator controls:

what happens each turn

Think of it as the system that applies the project rules and keeps the simulation progressing.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Main Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Simulator typically manages:

  • 🛩️ drone movement
  • ⏱️ turn progression
  • 📦 occupancy tracking
  • 🚦 rule enforcement
  • 📄 output generation

docs.title: 3️⃣ Simulation State
docs.text:
During execution, the Simulator keeps track of information such as:

current_turn = 7
active_drones = [...]
occupied_zones = {...}
occupied_links = {...}

The exact implementation may differ, but the idea remains the same:

store everything needed to make decisions

docs.title: 4️⃣ Drone Lifecycle
docs.text:
A drone normally moves through several states.

Created
   ↓
Waiting
   ↓
Moving
   ↓
Waiting (if required)
   ↓
Moving again
   ↓
Delivered

Not every drone will follow exactly the same route, but they all follow the same simulation rules.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Turn System
docs.text:
Fly-in is a:

turn-based simulation

Each turn represents a small step of time.

Example:

Turn 1
D1 -> A
D2 -> B

Turn 2
D1 -> C
D2 -> D

The Simulator evaluates every active drone and decides what can happen during that turn.

A simulation turn usually follows:

Check drones
    ↓
Validate movement
    ↓
Move drones
    ↓
Update occupancy
    ↓
Store turn result

The exact implementation may vary.

The important concept is:

all rules are applied consistently

docs.title: 6️⃣ Occupancy Tracking
docs.text:
To avoid conflicts, the Simulator usually tracks:

  • occupied zones
  • occupied connections
  • active movements

Example:

Zone A
 ├─ D1
 └─ D2

Without occupancy tracking, capacity rules cannot be enforced.

docs.title: 7️⃣ Capacity Management
docs.text:
Many simulations include limits such as:

Maximum drones per zone

or

Maximum drones per connection

Before a movement happens, those limits may need to be checked.

docs.title: 8️⃣ Waiting States
docs.text:
Sometimes a drone cannot move immediately.

Possible reasons:

  • destination unavailable
  • connection unavailable
  • special movement rules
  • temporary congestion

In these situations the Simulator may decide:

wait this turn

Waiting is a normal part of scheduling.

docs.title: 9️⃣ Movement Validation
docs.text:
Before moving a drone, several questions may need to be answered.

✅ Is the destination valid?

✅ Is there enough capacity?

✅ Can the connection be used?

✅ Does a valid route still exist?

Only after validation can the movement be applied.

docs.title: 🔟 Path Updates
docs.text:
Routes are not always permanent.

As the simulation changes:

  • occupancy changes
  • capacities change
  • available routes may change

Because of this, a route may need to be recalculated during execution.

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ End Conditions
docs.text:
The simulation ends when:

all drones have been delivered

Example:

D1 ✓
D2 ✓
D3 ✓
D4 ✓

No active drones remain.

docs.title: 1️⃣2️⃣ Simulator vs PathFinder
docs.text: These systems solve different problems.

docs.title: 🧭 PathFinder
docs.text:
Answers:

Where should the drone go?

docs.title: 🎮 Simulator
docs.text:
Answers:

Can the drone move right now?

Separating these responsibilities makes the project easier to understand, test and maintain.

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of the PathFinder as:

GPS navigation

It suggests routes.

Think of the Simulator as:

Air traffic control

It decides:

Who can move
When they can move
Whether the movement is valid

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is BFS?
docs.text: BFS means:

docs.title: Breadth-First Search
docs.text:
It is an algorithm used to:

  • traverse graphs
  • explore neighbors
  • find shortest paths
  • avoid infinite loops

docs.title: BFS explores:
docs.text:
layer by layer

NOT:

deep first

docs.title: 2️⃣ Graph Traversal
docs.text:
Imagine this graph:

        start
       /  |  \
      A   B   C
     /         \
    D           goal

BFS explores:

start
↓
A B C
↓
D goal

It explores all neighbors before going deeper.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Queue Concept
docs.text: BFS uses:

docs.title: FIFO Queue
docs.text:
FIFO means:

First In
First Out

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
ADD A
ADD B
ADD C

Queue:

[A, B, C]

Remove:

A

because:

A entered first

docs.title: 4️⃣ Why BFS uses deque
docs.text:
Python provides:

from collections import deque

deque means:

double-ended queue

docs.title: Useful Operations
docs.text:
append()
popleft()

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from collections import deque

queue = deque()

queue.append("A")
queue.append("B")
queue.append("C")

print(queue.popleft())

Output:

A

docs.title: 5️⃣ Layer-by-Layer Exploration
docs.text: BFS explores nodes in waves.

docs.title: Visual Example
docs.text:
LEVEL 0:
start

LEVEL 1:
A B C

LEVEL 2:
D goal

BFS always explores:

closest nodes first

docs.title: 6️⃣ Visual Simulation
docs.text:
Imagine water spreading:

start
  ↓
A B C
  ↓
D goal

BFS spreads equally in all directions.

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text: Explore neighbors first.

docs.title: Pseudo Code
docs.text:
CREATE queue
CREATE visited set
CREATE parent dictionary

ADD start zone to queue
MARK start as visited

WHILE queue not empty:

    current = queue.pop_left()

    IF current == goal:
        reconstruct path
        RETURN path

    FOR each connected zone:

        IF not visited:

            mark visited
            save parent
            add to queue

docs.title: 8️⃣ Visited Nodes
docs.text:
BFS MUST track visited nodes.

Why?

Because graphs may contain loops.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A -> B
↑    ↓
D <- C

Without visited tracking:

infinite loop

docs.title: Visited Set
docs.text:
Example:

visited = {
    "start",
    "A",
    "B"
}

docs.title: 9️⃣ Parent Tracking
docs.text:
BFS usually stores:

where each node came from

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
{
    "A": "start",
    "goal": "C"
}

This allows path reconstruction later.

docs.title: 🔟 Path Reconstruction
docs.text:
Once BFS reaches the goal:

go backwards using parents

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
goal
 ↑
 C
 ↑
 start

Reverse result:

start -> C -> goal

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Complexity
docs.text:
BFS complexity is usually:

O(V + E)

Where:

V = vertices (zones)
E = edges (connections)

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Good for:

  • unweighted graphs
  • same movement costs
  • shortest path in number of steps

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Good for:

  • weighted graphs
  • movement costs
  • cheapest total route

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
BFS thinks:

fewest movements

Dijkstra thinks:

lowest total cost

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ Fly-in Connection
docs.text:
Fly-in uses:

normal      = 1
priority    = 1
restricted  = 2
blocked     = impossible

This means:

not all paths cost the same

So:

BFS is useful to learn graph traversal

BUT:

Dijkstra fits Fly-in better

docs.title: 1️⃣4️⃣ Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of BFS like:

water spreading equally

or:

exploring closest rooms first

docs.title: Final Mental Image
docs.text:
BFS always asks:

"What is the closest unexplored node?"

NOT:

"What is the cheapest node?"

That is the BIG difference between:

BFS
vs
Dijkstra

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is DFS?
docs.text: DFS means:

docs.title: Depth-First Search
docs.text:
It is an algorithm used to:

  • traverse graphs
  • explore paths deeply
  • generate mazes
  • search recursive structures

docs.title: DFS explores:
docs.text:
go as deep as possible first

ONLY after:

backtrack

docs.title: 2️⃣ Graph Traversal
docs.text:
Imagine this graph:

        start
       /  |  \
      A   B   C
     /         \
    D           goal

DFS might explore:

start
↓
A
↓
D
↓
backtrack
↓
B
↓
C
↓
goal

docs.title: 3️⃣ DFS Mentality
docs.text:
DFS constantly thinks:

"Keep going deeper."

It prioritizes:

current branch exploration

before checking siblings.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Stack Behavior
docs.text: DFS behaves like:

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
A stack works like:

Last In
First Out

This is called:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
ADD A
ADD B
ADD C

Stack:

[A, B, C]

Remove:

C

because:

C was added last

docs.title: 5️⃣ Recursive Exploration
docs.text:
DFS is commonly implemented using:

recursion

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
visit(node)
    ↓
visit(neighbor)
    ↓
visit(neighbor)

docs.title: Recursion Flow
docs.text:
start
 ↓
A
 ↓
D
 ↓
end branch
 ↓
backtrack

docs.title: 6️⃣ Visual Example
docs.text:
Graph:

        start
       /     \
      A       B
     / \
    C   D

DFS exploration:

start
↓
A
↓
C
↓
backtrack
↓
D
↓
backtrack
↓
B

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text: Explore deeply before exploring widely.

docs.title: Recursive Pseudo Code
docs.text:
FUNCTION dfs(node):

    mark node as visited

    FOR each neighbor:

        IF neighbor not visited:

            dfs(neighbor)

docs.title: 8️⃣ Visited Nodes
docs.text:
DFS MUST track visited nodes.

Otherwise:

infinite recursion

may happen.

docs.title: Example Loop
docs.text:
A -> B
↑    ↓
D <- C

Without visited tracking:

A -> B -> C -> D -> A -> ...

forever 😄

docs.title: Visited Example
docs.text:
visited = {
    "start",
    "A",
    "B"
}

docs.title: 9️⃣ Recursive Backtracking
docs.text:
When DFS reaches:

end of branch

it:

returns backwards

This is called:

docs.title: Visual Example
docs.text:
start
 ↓
A
 ↓
C
 ↓
no neighbors
 ↓
backtrack to A
 ↓
explore D

docs.title: 🔟 Iterative DFS
docs.text:
DFS can also be implemented without recursion.

Using:

stack

docs.title: Iterative Pseudo Code
docs.text:
CREATE stack
CREATE visited set

PUSH start node

WHILE stack not empty:

    current = stack.pop()

    IF not visited:

        mark visited

        PUSH neighbors

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Complexity
docs.text:
DFS complexity is usually:

O(V + E)

Where:

V = vertices
E = edges

docs.title: DFS
docs.text:
Explores:

deep first

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Explores:

layer by layer

docs.title: DFS Mentality
docs.text: "Keep going deeper"

docs.title: BFS Mentality
docs.text: "Explore closest nodes first"

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ DFS in Maze Generation
docs.text: DFS is EXTREMELY popular for:

docs.title: maze generation
docs.text:
Because:

DFS naturally creates long corridors

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Your A-Maze-ing project uses:

Randomized DFS

to:

  • carve paths
  • explore cells
  • backtrack
  • generate perfect mazes

docs.title: DFS Maze Flow
docs.text:
current cell
    ↓
random neighbor
    ↓
carve wall
    ↓
continue deeper
    ↓
no neighbors?
    ↓
backtrack

docs.title: 1️⃣4️⃣ Fly-in Connection
docs.text:
DFS is useful in Fly-in for:

  • graph traversal learning
  • recursive exploration
  • understanding pathfinding
  • understanding backtracking

BUT:

DFS does NOT guarantee cheapest path

or:

shortest weighted route

That is why:

Dijkstra fits Fly-in better

docs.title: 1️⃣5️⃣ Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of DFS like:

exploring a cave

You:

  • keep moving deeper
  • follow one tunnel
  • only return when stuck

docs.title: Final Mental Image
docs.text:
DFS always asks:

"How far can I go from here?"

NOT:

"What is the closest node?"

That is the BIG difference between:

DFS
vs
BFS

docs.title: Path A
docs.text:
start -> restricted -> goal

Cost:

3 turns

docs.title: Path B
docs.text:
start -> normal -> normal -> goal

Cost:

3 turns

docs.title: Path C
docs.text:
start -> priority -> goal

Cost:

2 turns

Dijkstra chooses:

Path C

because:

lowest total cost

docs.title: 4️⃣ Priority Queue (heapq)
docs.text:
Dijkstra usually uses:

import heapq

because it constantly needs:

cheapest current node

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
queue = []

heapq.heappush(queue, (1, "A"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (5, "B"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (2, "C"))

docs.title: heapq automatically organizes:
docs.text: lowest cost first

docs.title: Then:
docs.text:
heapq.heappop(queue)

returns:

(1, "A")

docs.title: 5️⃣ Cost Tracking
docs.text:
Dijkstra stores:

best known cost for every node

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
costs = {
    "start": 0,
    "A": 1,
    "B": 4,
    "goal": 2
}

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text:
If a cheaper route is discovered:

update cost

docs.title: 6️⃣ Parent Tracking
docs.text:
Dijkstra also stores:

where each node came from

This allows:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
parents = {
    "A": "start",
    "goal": "A"
}

docs.title: 7️⃣ Visited Nodes
docs.text:
Dijkstra tracks visited nodes to avoid:

  • unnecessary processing
  • loops
  • recalculating explored routes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
visited = {
    "start",
    "A"
}

docs.title: 8️⃣ Dijkstra Logic
docs.text:
Dijkstra works by constantly exploring:

The currently cheapest known route

The algorithm starts at the initial node and gradually expands through the graph.

Every time it discovers a cheaper route to a node:

  • the cost is updated
  • the route information is updated
  • the node becomes a better candidate for exploration

docs.title: Cheapest-first exploration
docs.text:
Unlike BFS, Dijkstra does not explore based on:

arrival order

Instead, it explores based on:

lowest total cost

docs.title: Continuous optimization
docs.text:
The algorithm constantly compares:

old path cost
vs
new possible path cost

If the new route is cheaper:

replace old cost

docs.title: Progressive expansion
docs.text:
Dijkstra gradually expands through the graph:

start
 ↓
cheap neighbors
 ↓
slightly more expensive neighbors
 ↓
more expensive neighbors

The graph is explored in order of increasing total cost.

docs.title: Weighted movement
docs.text:
This is why Dijkstra is extremely useful for:

  • traffic systems
  • GPS navigation
  • weighted movement games
  • drone routing
  • path optimization

docs.title: 9️⃣ Path Reconstruction
docs.text:
Once the goal is reached:

walk backwards using parents

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
goal
 ↑
A
 ↑
start

Reverse result:

start -> A -> goal

docs.title: 🔟 Visual Example
docs.text:
Graph:

        start
       /     \
     (1)     (5)
     A         B
      \
      (1)
        \
        goal

docs.title: Exploration
docs.text:
Dijkstra sees:

A = cost 1
B = cost 5

It explores:

A first

because:

1 < 5

docs.title: Final Path
docs.text:
start -> A -> goal

Total cost:

2

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Fly-in Connection
docs.text: Fly-in is essentially:

docs.title: weighted graph pathfinding
docs.text:
because:

  • movement costs exist
  • restricted zones cost more
  • blocked zones are invalid
  • drones need optimized routes

docs.title: Dijkstra fits Fly-in because it:
docs.text:
• minimizes total movement cost
  • avoids blocked zones
  • supports weighted routing
  • generates optimal paths

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Explores:

fewest steps

Uses:

deque

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Explores:

lowest cost path

Uses:

heapq

docs.title: BFS
docs.text:
Good for:

  • unweighted graphs
  • same movement costs

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text:
Good for:

  • weighted graphs
  • route optimization
  • movement costs

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ Complexity
docs.text:
Typical Dijkstra complexity:

O((V + E) log V)

Where:

V = vertices
E = edges

docs.title: Why more expensive than BFS?
docs.text:
Because:

heapq sorting adds extra work

BUT:

weighted pathfinding requires it

docs.title: 1️⃣4️⃣ Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of Dijkstra like:

GPS route optimization

The algorithm constantly asks:

"What is currently the cheapest route?"

NOT:

"What was explored first?"

docs.title: BFS
docs.text: Closest room first

docs.title: Dijkstra
docs.text: Cheapest road first

docs.title: Queue vs Heap Queue (heapq)
docs.text: Understanding why BFS uses queues and Dijkstra uses priority queues.

docs.title: What is a Queue?
docs.text:
A queue works like:

First In
First Out

This is called:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
[A, B, C]

Removing:

A

because:

A entered first

docs.title: deque
docs.text:
Python provides:

from collections import deque

deque means:

double-ended queue

Useful operations:

append()
popleft()

docs.title: Why BFS uses deque
docs.text:
BFS explores:

level by level

Example:

start
 ├── A
 ├── B
 └── C

BFS processes:

A first
then B
then C

because:

they were inserted first

docs.title: BFS Behavior
docs.text:
ORDER OF ARRIVAL

This is PERFECT for:

deque

docs.title: What is heapq?
docs.text: heapq is Python's built-in:

docs.title: Difference from normal queue
docs.text:
A normal queue removes:

oldest element

A priority queue removes:

lowest cost first

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
You insert:

(5, "B")
(1, "A")
(3, "C")

docs.title: heapq automatically organizes:
docs.text:
lowest cost first

Then:

heapq.heappop(queue)

returns:

(1, "A")

EVEN IF:

A was inserted later

docs.title: Why Dijkstra uses heapq
docs.text:
Dijkstra constantly asks:

"What is the CHEAPEST current route?"

NOT:

"What was inserted first?"

That is why:

deque

is not ideal for Dijkstra.

docs.title: Dijkstra needs:
docs.text:
LOWEST COST FIRST

Which is exactly what:

heapq

does.

docs.title: Dijkstra Example
docs.text:
import heapq

queue = []

heapq.heappush(queue, (2, "roof1"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (1, "corridorA"))
heapq.heappush(queue, (5, "goal"))

print(heapq.heappop(queue))

Output:

(1, "corridorA")

docs.text:
ORDER OF ARRIVAL

A → B → C

LOWEST COST FIRST

1 → 2 → 5

Weighted Graph Pathfinding

Supermarket line

docs.title: Git Workflow for 42 School
docs.text:
A beginner-friendly guide to Git and GitHub workflows commonly used in 42 School projects.

This document explains: - branches - merge - pull - stash - reset - detached HEAD - GitHub workflow - common mistakes

Git is one of the most important tools for software development.

docs.title: What is Git?
docs.text:
Git is a version control system.

It tracks: - code changes - project history - branches - collaboration

Git allows developers to: - work safely - undo mistakes - collaborate efficiently

docs.title: What is GitHub?
docs.text:
GitHub is an online platform that hosts Git repositories.

GitHub allows: - remote backups - collaboration - pull requests - code sharing

docs.title: Basic Git Workflow
docs.text:
Most workflows follow this pattern:

Write code
→ git add
→ git commit
→ git push

docs.title: Repository
docs.text: A repository (repo) stores: - project files - commit history - branches

docs.title: Initializing a Repository
docs.text:
git init

Creates a local Git repository.

docs.title: Checking Repository Status
docs.text:
git status

One of the most important commands.

Shows: - modified files - staged files - current branch

docs.title: Adding Files
docs.text:
git add file.py

Adds a file to the staging area.

docs.title: Add Everything
docs.text:
git add .

Stages all modified files.

docs.title: Commit
docs.text: A commit is a saved snapshot of the project.

docs.title: Creating a Commit
docs.text: git commit -m "Added BFS solver"

docs.title: Good Commit Messages
docs.text:
Good:

Added config parser validation

Bad:

fix

Clear messages improve project history.

docs.title: Branches
docs.text:
Branches allow separate development paths.

Useful for: - new features - experiments - bug fixes

Without affecting the main project.

docs.title: Viewing Branches
docs.text: git branch

docs.title: Creating a Branch
docs.text: git branch feature-menu

docs.title: Switching Branches
docs.text: git checkout feature-menu

docs.title: Modern Alternative
docs.text: git switch feature-menu

docs.title: Create + Switch Together
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature-menu

or:

git switch -c feature-menu

docs.title: Why Branches Matter
docs.text:
Branches help: - isolate work - avoid breaking main - support teamwork

Very important in 42 group projects.

docs.title: Merge
docs.text: Merge combines branches together.

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
main
└── feature-menu

After development:

git checkout main
git merge feature-menu

This brings: - feature-menu changes - into main

docs.title: Merge Conflicts
docs.text:
Conflicts happen when: - two branches modify the same lines

Git cannot decide automatically.

docs.title: Conflict Example
docs.text:
<<<<<<< HEAD
old code
=======
new code
>>>>>>> feature

You must: - edit manually - choose correct code - remove conflict markers

docs.title: pull
docs.text: git pull downloads updates from GitHub.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
git pull

Equivalent to:

git fetch
git merge

docs.title: Why Pull Matters
docs.text:
Always pull before: - pushing - merging - starting work

Especially in team projects.

docs.title: push
docs.text: Uploads local commits to GitHub.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: git push

docs.title: First Push of New Branch
docs.text: git push -u origin feature-menu

docs.title: origin
docs.text: origin usually means: - the GitHub repository

docs.title: stash
docs.text:
git stash temporarily saves unfinished work.

Very useful when: - changing branches - pulling updates - testing another branch

docs.title: Example
docs.text: git stash

docs.title: Restoring Stash
docs.text: git stash pop

docs.title: Viewing Stashes
docs.text: git stash list

docs.title: reset
docs.text:
git reset moves commits or unstages changes.

Dangerous if used incorrectly.

docs.title: Unstage Files
docs.text: git reset

docs.title: Reset to Previous Commit
docs.text:
git reset --hard HEAD~1

Removes: - last commit - changes permanently

Be careful.

docs.title: Soft Reset
docs.text:
git reset --soft HEAD~1

Removes commit: - keeps file changes

Safer than --hard.

docs.title: Detached HEAD
docs.text: A detached HEAD means: - Git is not on a branch - you are viewing a specific commit

docs.title: Common Cause
docs.text:
git checkout abc123

Where: - abc123 is a commit hash

docs.title: Why Detached HEAD is Dangerous
docs.text: Commits made here may become lost.

docs.title: Fix Detached HEAD
docs.text:
Return to a branch:

git checkout main

or:

git switch main

docs.title: Clone Repository
docs.text: Downloads a GitHub repository locally.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: git clone https://github.com/user/project.git

docs.title: GitHub Workflow for 42
docs.text: Common workflow:

docs.title: Step 1
docs.text:
Clone project:

git clone REPOSITORY_URL

docs.title: Step 2
docs.text:
Enter project:

cd project

docs.title: Step 3
docs.text:
Create branch:

git checkout -b sara-feature

docs.title: Step 4
docs.text: Work normally.

docs.title: Step 5
docs.text:
Add files:

git add .

docs.title: Step 6
docs.text:
Commit:

git commit -m "Added MLX rendering"

docs.title: Step 7
docs.text:
Push branch:

git push -u origin sara-feature

docs.title: Step 8
docs.text: Merge later into main.

docs.title: "Your branch has diverged"
docs.text:
Means: - local and remote history differ

Usually fixed with:

git pull

or: - merge/rebase

docs.title: "nothing to commit"
docs.text: Means: - no modified files exist

docs.title: Accidentally Working on main
docs.text:
Very common mistake.

Fix: - create branch - move work there

docs.title: Detached HEAD Confusion
docs.text:
Students often checkout commits directly.

Always verify current branch:

git branch

docs.title: Pull Before Push
docs.text:
Always:

git pull
git push

Especially in teams.

docs.title: Useful Commands Summary
docs.text: Command Purpose git status View repository state git add Stage files git commit Save snapshot git push Upload to GitHub git pull Download updates git branch View branches git checkout Switch branches git stash Temporarily save work git reset Undo/reset changes

docs.title: Recommended .gitignore
docs.text: Do not upload: - virtual environments - cache files - compiled Python files

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Commit frequently
  • Use meaningful commit messages
  • Pull before pushing
  • Avoid working directly on main
  • Use branches for features
  • Verify branch before committing
  • Avoid dangerous reset commands unless necessary

docs.title: Good Branch Names
docs.text:
Good:

feature-menu
mlx-renderer
config-parser

Bad:

test
stuff
aaa

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
Git is an essential skill for modern software development.

Strong Git knowledge helps: - teamwork - debugging - project organization - safer development - version tracking

Git becomes especially important in: - 42 group projects - MLX projects - large codebases - collaborative development

Understanding Git deeply will save countless hours during development.
