docs.title: 📖 What is a Package?
docs.text:
A package is a folder containing Python modules.

Packages help: - organize code - separate logic - structure large projects - improve readability

docs.title: 📦 What is a Module?
docs.text:
A module is simply:

a Python file

Example:

utils.py

This file itself is a module.

docs.title: 🧱 Basic Package Structure
docs.text:
project/
│
├── main.py
├── parser/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── config_parser.py
│   └── validator.py
│
└── render/
    ├── __init__.py
    └── draw.py

docs.title: Import Entire Module
docs.text:
import math

Usage:

math.sqrt(25)

docs.title: Import Specific Function
docs.text:
from math import sqrt

Usage:

sqrt(25)

docs.title: 📌 init.py
docs.text:
__init__.py tells Python that a folder should behave like a package.

Without it, imports may fail in some project structures.

docs.title: 🌿 Absolute Imports
docs.text: Absolute imports start from the project root.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: from parser.config_parser import parse_config

docs.title: 🔗 Relative Imports
docs.text: Relative imports use dots (.).

docs.title: Example
docs.text: from .validator import validate_config

docs.title: 🛠️ Organizing Large Projects
docs.text:
Example organization:

project/
│
├── core/
├── parsing/
├── render/
├── utils/
├── tests/
└── assets/
└── main.py

And each folder would have a init.py file to make it a package.

docs.title: 📌 init.py Contents
docs.text:
In many Python projects, __init__.py is often left empty.

Example:

# __init__.py

This is completely normal and commonly used only to mark the folder as a package.

docs.title: Why Leave It Empty?
docs.text: An empty __init__.py: - keeps the package simple - avoids unnecessary imports - helps Python recognize the directory as a package

docs.title: What Can Be Inside init.py?
docs.text: Although often empty, it may also contain: - package imports - helper variables - package initialization code - exposed public functions/classes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from .config_parser import parse_config
from .validator import validate_config

This allows imports like:

from parser import parse_config

instead of:

from parser.config_parser import parse_config

docs.title: Why This is Useful
docs.text:
This can: - simplify imports - expose public APIs - improve package organization

Very common in: - libraries - frameworks - large Python projects

docs.title: Good Practice
docs.text:
For beginner projects: - keeping __init__.py empty is completely fine

As projects grow larger: - it may be used to organize package exports and initialization logic

docs.title: ❌ Missing init.py
docs.text:
Problem:

ModuleNotFoundError

docs.title: ❌ Circular Imports
docs.text:
Example:

# a.py imports b.py
# b.py imports a.py

docs.title: ❌ Running Files Directly
docs.text:
Bad:

python3 parser/config_parser.py

Better:

python3 main.py

docs.title: 📚 Final Notes
docs.text:
Understanding packages is extremely important for modern Python development.

Good package organization helps: - readability - maintainability - debugging - scalability - teamwork

docs.title: Python Virtual Environments Guide
docs.text:
A beginner-friendly guide to Python virtual environments and package management.

This document explains: - venv - activation - pip - requirements.txt - Poetry - PEP 668 - common problems in 42 School environments

Virtual environments are essential for modern Python development.

docs.title: What is a Virtual Environment?
docs.text:
A virtual environment is an isolated Python environment.

It allows each project to: - have its own packages - use different library versions - avoid conflicts with system Python

Without virtual environments: - projects may break each other - package versions may conflict - system Python can become unstable

docs.title: Why Use Virtual Environments?
docs.text: Virtual environments help: - isolate dependencies - create reproducible projects - avoid permission issues - protect system Python - simplify collaboration

docs.title: Creating a Virtual Environment
docs.text: Python includes venv by default.

docs.title: Create venv
docs.text:
python3 -m venv venv

This creates a folder called:

venv/

Containing: - Python executable - pip - installed packages - activation scripts

docs.title: Common Naming
docs.text:
Most projects use:

venv/

Other common names: - .venv - env

docs.title: Activating the Virtual Environment
docs.text: The environment must be activated before installing packages.

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text: source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: Windows CMD
docs.text: venv\Scripts\activate.bat

docs.title: Windows PowerShell
docs.text: venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

docs.title: Successful Activation
docs.text:
The terminal usually changes.

Example:

(venv) user@computer:~/project$

This means the environment is active.

docs.title: Checking the Python Path
docs.text:
Use:

which python3

Example output:

/home/user/project/venv/bin/python3

If it shows:

/usr/bin/python3

then the venv is probably NOT activated.

docs.title: Deactivating the Environment
docs.text:
deactivate

This returns to the system Python.

docs.title: pip
docs.text:
pip is Python's package manager.

Used for: - installing packages - updating packages - removing packages

docs.title: Installing Packages
docs.text: pip install pydantic

docs.title: Installing Specific Versions
docs.text: pip install "pydantic>=2"

docs.title: Installing Multiple Packages
docs.text: pip install flake8 mypy requests

docs.title: Viewing Installed Packages
docs.text: pip list

docs.title: requirements.txt
docs.text:
requirements.txt stores project dependencies.

Useful for: - reproducibility - collaboration - deployment

docs.title: Creating requirements.txt
docs.text: pip freeze > requirements.txt

docs.title: Example requirements.txt
docs.text:
pydantic==2.11.0
mypy==1.15.0
flake8==7.2.0

docs.title: Installing from requirements.txt
docs.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Why requirements.txt Matters
docs.text: Without it: - other users may install wrong versions - projects may stop working - environments become inconsistent

docs.title: Poetry
docs.text:
Poetry is a more advanced Python dependency manager.

It replaces: - pip - requirements.txt - manual dependency handling

docs.title: Poetry Features
docs.text: Poetry: - manages dependencies - creates virtual environments - handles package versions - simplifies project management

docs.title: Installing Poetry
docs.text:
Linux/macOS:

curl -sSL https://install.python-poetry.org | python3 -

docs.title: Creating a Poetry Project
docs.text: poetry init

docs.title: Installing Dependencies
docs.text: poetry add pydantic

docs.title: Activating Poetry Environment
docs.text: poetry shell

docs.title: Installing from pyproject.toml
docs.text: poetry install

docs.title: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
Poetry stores dependencies in:

pyproject.toml

Instead of: - requirements.txt

docs.title: Example pyproject.toml
docs.text:
[tool.poetry.dependencies]
python = "^3.10"
pydantic = "^2.11"

docs.title: PEP 668
docs.text:
PEP 668 protects system Python environments.

Modern Linux distributions may block:

pip install package

outside virtual environments.

docs.title: Common Error
docs.text:
externally-managed-environment

This is extremely common on: - Ubuntu - Debian - 42 machines

docs.title: Why This Happens
docs.text: The operating system wants to protect: - system Python - system packages - package manager integrity

docs.title: Recommended Solution
docs.text:
Use virtual environments:

python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

Then install packages normally.

docs.title: Alternative Solution
docs.text:
Sometimes users install locally:

pip install --user package

But virtual environments are cleaner.

docs.title: Common Problems in 42 School
docs.text: 42 environments often have: - limited permissions - restricted system access - outdated packages - disk quota issues

docs.title: Common Problem: No Permission
docs.text:
Example:

Permission denied

Usually solved by: - using venv - avoiding sudo

docs.title: Common Problem: PEP 668
docs.text:
Example:

externally-managed-environment

Solution: - create a venv

docs.title: Common Problem: No Space Left on Device
docs.text:
Example:

OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device

Very common in: - /tmp - home directories - cached pip files

docs.title: Possible Solutions
docs.text:
Clean pip cache:

pip cache purge

Remove unused venvs:

rm -rf venv

docs.title: Common Problem: Wrong Python Version
docs.text:
Check version:

python3 --version

docs.title: Common Problem: Missing Packages
docs.text:
Example:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pydantic'

Usually means: - venv not activated - package not installed

docs.title: Common Problem: which python3
docs.text:
If this returns:

/usr/bin/python3

the venv is probably inactive.

docs.title: Common Problem: Broken venv
docs.text:
Sometimes virtual environments break.

Fix:

rm -rf venv
python3 -m venv venv

docs.title: Step 1
docs.text:
Create project folder:

mkdir my_project
cd my_project

docs.title: Step 2
docs.text:
Create virtual environment:

python3 -m venv venv

docs.title: Step 3
docs.text:
Activate environment:

source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: Step 4
docs.text:
Install dependencies:

pip install pydantic mypy flake8

docs.title: Step 5
docs.text:
Save dependencies:

pip freeze > requirements.txt

docs.title: Recommended .gitignore
docs.text:
Do NOT upload virtual environments to GitHub.

Example:

venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Always use virtual environments
  • Never commit venv folders
  • Keep requirements.txt updated
  • Prefer precise package versions
  • Activate venv before working
  • Avoid using sudo pip install

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Tool / Concept Purpose venv Isolated Python environment pip Package manager requirements.txt Stores dependencies Poetry Advanced dependency manager PEP 668 Protects system Python activate Enables virtual environment

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
Virtual environments are one of the most important parts of modern Python development.

Understanding them helps avoid: - dependency conflicts - broken projects - permission issues - package problems

This becomes especially important in: - 42 projects - team environments - APIs - large Python applications - deployment systems

docs.title: 🚨 Python Exceptions & Try/Except Guide
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly documentation about Python exceptions, error handling, try/except blocks, and debugging practices.

This document explains: - exceptions - errors - try - except - finally - else - custom exceptions - common debugging patterns

docs.title: 📚 Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 📖 What are Exceptions?
  • ⚠️ Common Python Errors
  • 🛠️ try / except
  • 📦 Catching Specific Exceptions
  • 🔄 else
  • 🧹 finally
  • 🚨 Raising Exceptions
  • 🏗️ Custom Exceptions
  • 🎮 Real 42 Examples
  • ⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes
  • 📚 Best Practices
  • 📚 Final Notes

docs.title: 📖 What are Exceptions?
docs.text:
Exceptions are: - runtime errors - unexpected problems during execution

When an exception happens: - Python stops normal execution

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
print(10 / 0)

Output:

ZeroDivisionError

docs.title: Why Exceptions Matter
docs.text:
Exceptions help: - detect problems - avoid crashes - improve debugging - handle invalid data safely

Very important in: - parsers - APIs - MLX projects - file systems - user input validation

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Python Errors
docs.text: Exception Meaning ValueError Invalid value TypeError Wrong type ZeroDivisionError Division by zero FileNotFoundError File missing KeyError Missing dictionary key IndexError Invalid list index

docs.title: 🛠️ try / except
docs.text:
try allows Python to: - attempt risky code

except handles: - the error safely

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
try:
    number = int(input("Number: "))
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid number")

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Python: 1. runs the try block 2. checks for exceptions 3. jumps to except if an error happens

docs.title: 📦 Catching Specific Exceptions
docs.text: Good practice: - catch specific errors

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
try:
    value = int("abc")

except ValueError:
    print("Conversion failed")

docs.title: Avoid Catching Everything
docs.text:
Bad:

except:
    print("Error")

This hides: - debugging information - unexpected problems

docs.title: Better Version
docs.text: except ValueError:

docs.title: 🔄 else
docs.text: else runs ONLY if: - no exception happened

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
try:
    value = int("42")

except ValueError:
    print("Invalid")

else:
    print("Success")

docs.title: 🧹 finally
docs.text:
finally ALWAYS runs.

Even if: - exceptions happen

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
try:
    file = open("test.txt")

except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Missing file")

finally:
    print("Finished")

docs.title: Why finally is Useful
docs.text: Useful for: - closing files - cleanup - freeing resources

docs.title: 🚨 Raising Exceptions
docs.text:
You can manually create exceptions using:

raise

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
age = -5

if age < 0:
    raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")

docs.title: Why raise is Useful
docs.text: Useful for: - validation - parsers - enforcing rules - debugging

docs.title: 🏗️ Custom Exceptions
docs.text: You can create your own exception classes.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class MapParserError(Exception):
    pass

docs.title: Raising the Custom Exception
docs.text: raise MapParserError("Invalid map format")

docs.title: Why Custom Exceptions Matter
docs.text:
They help: - organize errors - improve debugging - create cleaner architecture

Very common in: - parsers - APIs - game engines

docs.title: Config Validation
docs.text:
if width <= 0:
    raise ValueError("Width must be positive")

docs.title: Missing File
docs.text: except FileNotFoundError:

docs.title: Parser Error
docs.text:
class ConfigError(Exception):
    pass

docs.title: Invalid Coordinates
docs.text:
raise MapParserError(
    "Invalid coordinates"
)

docs.title: ❌ Catching All Exceptions
docs.text:
Bad:

except:

Too broad.

docs.title: ❌ Ignoring Exceptions
docs.text:
Bad:

except ValueError:
    pass

Can hide bugs.

docs.title: ❌ Using Exceptions for Normal Logic
docs.text:
Exceptions should handle: - unexpected problems

Not: - normal flow control

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting finally Cleanup
docs.text: Files/resources may remain open.

docs.title: 📚 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Catch specific exceptions
  • Use meaningful error messages
  • Create custom exceptions for large projects
  • Avoid empty except
  • Keep exception handling readable
  • Use finally for cleanup

docs.title: 🧠 Why Exceptions Matter
docs.text:
Good exception handling helps: - debugging - reliability - maintainability - cleaner architecture

Exceptions become essential in: - parsers - file systems - APIs - MLX projects - validation systems

docs.title: 📚 Final Notes
docs.text:
Understanding exceptions is one of the most important Python skills.

Good error handling creates: - safer applications - cleaner debugging - better architecture - more maintainable projects

Strong exception handling becomes increasingly important in larger projects.

docs.title: 🔍 Python Traceback & Debugging Guide
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly documentation about Python tracebacks, exception debugging, and useful debugging patterns.

This document explains: - what a traceback is - how exceptions work - traceback module - extracting function names - debugging helpers - useful debugging patterns

docs.title: 📚 Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 📖 What is a Traceback?
  • ⚠️ Basic Exception Output
  • 🧠 Using traceback
  • 📦 extract_tb()
  • 🎯 Getting the Failed Function Name
  • 🛠️ Useful Debugging Patterns
  • 🎮 Real 42 Examples
  • ⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes
  • 📚 Best Practices
  • 📚 Final Notes

docs.title: 📖 What is a Traceback?
docs.text:
A traceback is: - Python's error report

It shows: - where the error happened - which functions were called - the exception type - the error message

docs.title: Example Traceback
docs.text:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 10, in <module>
    divide(10, 0)
  File "main.py", line 5, in divide
    return a / b

ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

docs.title: ⚠️ Basic Exception Output
docs.text:
try:
    value = 10 / 0

except Exception as e:
    print(type(e).__name__)
    print(e)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
ZeroDivisionError
division by zero

docs.title: 🧠 Using traceback
docs.text:
import traceback

Useful for: - advanced debugging - custom error displays - logging

docs.title: 📦 extract_tb()
docs.text: traceback.extract_tb() extracts traceback information.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import traceback

try:
    value = 10 / 0

except Exception as e:

    tb = traceback.extract_tb(e.__traceback__)

    print(tb)

docs.title: 🎯 Getting the Failed Function Name
docs.text:
import traceback

try:
    value = 10 / 0

except Exception as e:

    tb = traceback.extract_tb(e.__traceback__)

    print(f"Failed on the function: {tb[-1].name}")
    print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
Failed on the function: divide
ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

docs.title: Showing the Exception Type
docs.text: print(type(e).__name__)

docs.title: Showing the Exception Message
docs.text: print(e)

docs.title: Combining Both
docs.text: print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")

docs.title: Parser Errors
docs.text:
except Exception as e:

    tb = traceback.extract_tb(e.__traceback__)

    print(f"Failed on function: {tb[-1].name}")

docs.title: Invalid Config
docs.text: ValueError: Invalid WIDTH

docs.title: Invalid Coordinates
docs.text: MapParserError: Invalid coordinates

docs.title: ❌ Using Bare except
docs.text:
Bad:

except:

docs.title: ❌ Ignoring Tracebacks
docs.text: The traceback usually shows: - the REAL problem location

docs.title: ❌ Printing Huge Tracebacks Everywhere
docs.text: Too much debugging output can: - clutter logs - make debugging harder

docs.title: 📚 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Read traceback lines carefully
  • Look at the LAST function call first
  • Print exception types clearly
  • Use meaningful error messages
  • Add debugging progressively

docs.title: 📚 Final Notes
docs.text:
Understanding tracebacks is essential for Python development.

Good traceback handling helps: - locate bugs quickly - improve error reporting - simplify debugging

docs.title: Functions Are First-Class Objects
docs.text:
In Python, functions are treated like any other object.

This means functions can: - be stored in variables - be passed as arguments - be returned from other functions - be stored inside lists/dictionaries

docs.title: Function Object vs Function Call
docs.text: This is one of the most important concepts in functional programming.

docs.title: Function Object
docs.text:
def greet():
    return "Hello"


print(greet)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
<function greet at 0x...>

Here we are referencing the function itself.

No execution happens.

docs.title: Function Call
docs.text:
def greet():
    return "Hello"


print(greet())

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Hello

The parentheses () execute the function.

docs.title: Visual Difference
docs.text: Code Meaning greet Function object greet() Function call / execution

docs.title: Storing Functions in Variables
docs.text:
def fireball():
    return "Fireball cast!"


spell = fireball

print(spell())

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Fireball cast!

The variable stores the function itself.

docs.title: Passing Functions as Arguments
docs.text: Functions can be passed into other functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def greet(name):
    return f"Hello {name}"


def execute(func, value):
    return func(value)


print(execute(greet, "Sara"))

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Hello Sara

docs.title: Why Is This Powerful?
docs.text: This allows: - reusable logic - decorators - callbacks - flexible systems - functional programming patterns

docs.title: Returning Functions
docs.text: Functions can also return other functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def multiplier(x):

    def inner(y):
        return x * y

    return inner


double = multiplier(2)

print(double(5))

docs.title: Output
docs.text: 10

docs.title: Visual Representation
docs.text:
multiplier(2)
│
└── returns inner()
        │
        └── remembers x = 2

This is the basis of closures.

docs.title: What Is Callable?
docs.text:
Callable is a type hint used for functions.

It comes from:

from collections.abc import Callable

docs.title: Basic Callable Example
docs.text:
from collections.abc import Callable


def execute(func: Callable, value: int) -> int:
    return func(value)

docs.title: Callable With Parameters and Return Types
docs.text: You can specify: - argument types - return type

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: Callable[[arg1_type, arg2_type], return_type]

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from collections.abc import Callable


def operation(func: Callable[[int, int], int]) -> int:
    return func(2, 3)

docs.title: What Does callable() Do?
docs.text: callable() checks if an object can be called like a function.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def greet():
    pass


print(callable(greet))
print(callable(42))

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
True
False

docs.title: Objects Can Also Be Callable
docs.text: Classes using __call__() become callable.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Spell:

    def __call__(self):
        return "Magic!"


spell = Spell()

print(callable(spell))
print(spell())

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
True
Magic!

docs.title: Functions Inside Data Structures
docs.text: Functions can be stored inside: - lists - tuples - dictionaries

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def fire():
    return "Fire"


def ice():
    return "Ice"


spells = {
    "fire": fire,
    "ice": ice
}

print(spells["fire"]())

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Fire

docs.title: Returning a Function Call Instead of the Function
docs.text:
Wrong:

return greet()

Correct:

return greet

docs.title: Forgetting Parentheses
docs.text:
Wrong:

print(greet)

Correct:

print(greet())

docs.title: Real Use Cases
docs.text: These concepts are heavily used in: - decorators - callbacks - event systems - retry systems - Flask/FastAPI routes - functional programming - ML libraries

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Functions are first-class objects in Python
  • Functions can be passed, stored, and returned
  • Callable is used for function type hints
  • callable() checks if an object can be executed
  • function and function() are very different
  • Closures and decorators rely heavily on these concepts

docs.title: What is a Closure?
docs.text:
A closure is a function that remembers variables from the scope where it was created, even after that outer function has finished running.

Closures allow functions to keep a private internal state without using classes or global variables.

docs.title: Basic Closure Example
docs.text:
def outer():
    message = "Hello"

    def inner():
        return message

    return inner


func = outer()

print(func())

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Hello

docs.title: Why Does This Work?
docs.text:
Normally, local variables disappear after a function ends.

But here:

message = "Hello"

is remembered by:

inner()

Even after outer() has finished.

That remembered environment is called a:

docs.title: Lexical Scoping
docs.text:
Python uses lexical scoping.

This means: - inner functions can access variables from outer scopes - variable lookup depends on where the function was defined

docs.title: Scope Order (LEGB Rule)
docs.text:
Python searches variables in this order:

Scope Meaning Local Current function Enclosing Outer function Global File-level variables Built-in Python built-ins

docs.title: Closure Memory Example
docs.text:
def counter():
    count = 0

    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count

    return increment


c = counter()

print(c())
print(c())
print(c())

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
1
2
3

docs.title: Visual Representation
docs.text:
counter()
│
├── count = 0
│
└── returns increment()
        │
        └── remembers count variable

The function keeps access to count even after counter() has ended.

docs.title: What Does nonlocal Do?
docs.text:
nonlocal allows modifying variables from the enclosing function scope.

Without nonlocal, Python creates a new local variable instead.

docs.title: Without nonlocal
docs.text:
def counter():
    count = 0

    def increment():
        count += 1
        return count

    return increment

docs.title: Error
docs.text:
UnboundLocalError:
local variable 'count' referenced before assignment

Python thinks count is local to increment().

docs.title: Correct Version With nonlocal
docs.text:
def counter():
    count = 0

    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count

    return increment

docs.title: global vs nonlocal
docs.text: global nonlocal Modifies global variables Modifies enclosing scope variables Works at file/module level Works inside nested functions Less safe Safer for closures Shared everywhere Private to closure

docs.title: Example Using global
docs.text:
count = 0

def increment():
    global count
    count += 1

This changes a variable shared by the whole program.

docs.title: Example Using nonlocal
docs.text:
def counter():
    count = 0

    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count

    return increment

This creates private persistent state.

docs.title: Persistent State Without Classes
docs.text: Closures are often used instead of classes for small stateful systems.

docs.title: Closure Version
docs.text:
def accumulator(start):
    total = start

    def add(value):
        nonlocal total
        total += value
        return total

    return add


acc = accumulator(100)

print(acc(20))
print(acc(30))

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
120
150

docs.title: Equivalent Class Version
docs.text:
class Accumulator:
    def __init__(self, start):
        self.total = start

    def add(self, value):
        self.total += value
        return self.total

Closures provide a lighter functional alternative.

docs.title: Real Use Cases for Closures
docs.text: Closures are commonly used for: - decorators - counters - caching systems - state tracking - callback functions - factories - retry systems

docs.title: Forgetting nonlocal
docs.text:
count += 1

without:

nonlocal count

docs.title: Returning the Function Call Instead of the Function
docs.text:
Wrong:

return increment()

Correct:

return increment

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Closures remember variables from outer scopes
  • Python uses lexical scoping
  • nonlocal modifies enclosing scope variables
  • Closures can maintain persistent state
  • Closures are powerful alternatives to small classes
  • Closures are heavily used in decorators and functional programming

docs.title: What Is a Decorator?
docs.text:
A decorator is a function that modifies another function.

Decorators allow adding extra behavior: - logging - timing - validation - retry systems - permissions - caching

Without changing the original function.

docs.title: Basic Decorator Structure
docs.text:
def decorator(func):

    def wrapper():
        print("Before function")

        result = func()

        print("After function")

        return result

    return wrapper

docs.title: Applying the Decorator
docs.text:
@decorator
def greet():
    print("Hello")

Equivalent to:

greet = decorator(greet)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Before function
Hello
After function

docs.title: Why Are Decorators Useful?
docs.text: They separate concerns: - business logic stays clean - reusable behavior stays outside functions

docs.title: Decorator With Arguments
docs.text: Most decorators need *args and **kwargs.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def logger(func):

    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        print("Function called")

        return func(*args, **kwargs)

    return wrapper

docs.title: Timing Decorator
docs.text:
import time


def timer(func):

    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()

        result = func(*args, **kwargs)

        end = time.time()

        print(f"Executed in {end - start:.3f}s")

        return result

    return wrapper

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
@timer
def slow_spell():
    time.sleep(1)

docs.title: Validation Decorator
docs.text:
def validate_power(min_power):

    def decorator(func):

        def wrapper(power):
            if power < min_power:
                return "Insufficient power"

            return func(power)

        return wrapper

    return decorator

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
@validate_power(10)
def cast_spell(power):
    return f"Spell cast with {power}"

docs.title: Decorator Factories
docs.text: Decorators with parameters are called:

docs.title: → Decorator Factories
docs.text: Because they create decorators dynamically.

docs.title: Retry Decorator
docs.text:
def retry(max_attempts):

    def decorator(func):

        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):

            for attempt in range(max_attempts):

                try:
                    return func(*args, **kwargs)

                except Exception:
                    print("Retrying...")

            return "Failed"

        return wrapper

    return decorator

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
@retry(3)
def unstable_spell():
    raise ValueError("Boom")

docs.title: Stacking Decorators
docs.text: Multiple decorators can be combined.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@timer
@logger
def fireball():
    print("Fireball!")

Equivalent to:

fireball = timer(logger(fireball))

docs.title: Execution Order
docs.text: Decorators execute from bottom to top.

docs.title: Why Use functools.wraps?
docs.text: Without wraps, decorators replace: - function name - docstring - metadata

docs.title: Problem Example
docs.text:
def decorator(func):

    def wrapper():
        return func()

    return wrapper

print(greet.__name__)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
wrapper

The original name is lost.

docs.title: Correct Version With wraps
docs.text:
from functools import wraps


def decorator(func):

    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        return func(*args, **kwargs)

    return wrapper

Now metadata is preserved.

docs.title: Decorators on Methods
docs.text: Decorators also work on class methods.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Mage:

    @staticmethod
    def validate(name):
        return len(name) > 2

docs.title: Forgetting to Return the Wrapper
docs.text:
Wrong:

return func

Correct:

return wrapper

docs.title: Forgetting *args and **kwargs
docs.text:
Wrong:

def wrapper():

Correct:

def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):

docs.title: Real Use Cases
docs.text: Decorators are heavily used in: - Flask - FastAPI - Django - testing frameworks - logging systems - caching systems - authentication - retry systems

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Decorators modify function behavior
  • They help separate concerns
  • @decorator is syntactic sugar
  • wraps preserves metadata
  • Decorators can accept parameters
  • Multiple decorators can be stacked
  • Decorators are extremely common in modern Python

docs.title: 🧩 Python Lambda Functions Guide
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly documentation about Python lambda functions, anonymous functions, and common functional programming patterns.

This document explains: - what lambda functions are - lambda syntax - common use cases - map() - filter() - sorted() - practical examples - common mistakes

docs.title: 📚 Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 📖 What is a Lambda Function?
  • 🧱 Basic Syntax
  • ⚙️ Lambda vs Normal Functions
  • 📦 Returning Values
  • 🔗 Using Multiple Arguments
  • 🗂️ Lambda with sorted()
  • 🧪 Lambda with map()
  • 🔍 Lambda with filter()
  • 🎮 Real 42 Examples
  • ⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes
  • 📚 Best Practices
  • 📚 Final Notes

docs.title: 📖 What is a Lambda Function?
docs.text:
A lambda function is: - a small anonymous function - created in a single line - often used for quick operations

Unlike normal functions: - lambda functions usually do not have names

docs.title: 🧱 Basic Syntax
docs.text: lambda arguments: expression

docs.title: Simple Example
docs.text:
square = lambda x: x * x

print(square(5))

Output:

25

docs.title: Equivalent Normal Function
docs.text:
def square(x):
    return x * x

Both functions behave the same way.

docs.title: Lambda Function
docs.text: lambda x: x + 1

docs.title: Normal Function
docs.text:
def add_one(x):
    return x + 1

docs.title: 📦 Returning Values
docs.text: Lambda functions automatically return the expression result.

docs.title: 🔗 Using Multiple Arguments
docs.text: add = lambda a, b, c: a + b + c

docs.title: 🗂️ Lambda with sorted()
docs.text: players.sort(key=lambda player: player[1])

docs.title: 🧪 Lambda with map()
docs.text:
numbers = [1, 2, 3]

result = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, numbers))

docs.title: 🔍 Lambda with filter()
docs.text:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]

evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, numbers))

docs.title: Sorting Coordinates
docs.text: positions.sort(key=lambda pos: pos[0])

docs.title: Filtering Valid Zones
docs.text: valid_zones = list(filter(lambda zone: zone.active, zones))

docs.title: ❌ Trying to Use Multiple Lines
docs.text:
Wrong:

lambda x:
    x + 1

Lambda functions must stay on ONE line.

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting list()
docs.text:
map() and filter() return iterators.

Usually convert them:

list(map(...))

docs.title: 📚 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use lambdas for short operations
  • Prefer normal functions for complex logic
  • Keep lambdas readable
  • Avoid nested lambdas

docs.title: 📚 Final Notes
docs.text:
Lambda functions are extremely common in modern Python.

They are especially useful for: - quick transformations - sorting - filtering - concise operations

docs.title: 🧠 Python Class Methods & Attributes Guide
docs.text:
Beginner-friendly documentation about instance methods, class methods, static methods, attributes, and common object-oriented programming patterns in Python.

This document explains: - instance methods - class methods - static methods - instance attributes - class attributes - self - cls - common use cases - practical examples

docs.title: 📚 Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 📖 What are Methods?
  • 📌 self
  • 📦 Instance Attributes
  • 🏛️ Class Attributes
  • ⚙️ Instance Methods
  • 🏷️ Class Methods
  • 🔗 cls
  • 🧩 Static Methods
  • 📊 Method Comparison Table
  • 🏗️ Factory Methods
  • 🎮 Real 42 Examples
  • ⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes
  • 📚 Best Practices
  • 📚 Final Notes

docs.title: 📖 What are Methods?
docs.text:
Methods are: - functions inside classes

Methods help objects: - perform actions - manipulate data - organize behavior

docs.title: 📌 self
docs.text:
self refers to: - the current object instance

Used to access: - object attributes - object methods

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Player:

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

docs.title: 📦 Instance Attributes
docs.text:
Instance attributes belong to: - individual objects

Each object stores its own values.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Drone:

    def __init__(self, battery):
        self.battery = battery

docs.title: Creating Objects
docs.text:
drone1 = Drone(100)
drone2 = Drone(50)

Each object has: - different battery values

docs.title: 🏛️ Class Attributes
docs.text:
Class attributes belong to: - the class itself

Shared by ALL objects.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Drone:

    max_speed = 10

docs.title: Accessing Class Attributes
docs.text:
print(Drone.max_speed)

or:

drone = Drone()

print(drone.max_speed)

docs.title: Why Class Attributes Are Useful
docs.text: Useful for: - constants - shared settings - counters - global configuration

docs.title: ⚙️ Instance Methods
docs.text:
Instance methods work with: - object data

They receive: - self

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Player:

    def jump(self):
        print("Jump!")

docs.title: Using the Method
docs.text:
player = Player()

player.jump()

docs.title: When to Use Instance Methods
docs.text: Use instance methods when: - behavior depends on object data

docs.title: 🏷️ Class Methods
docs.text:
Class methods work with: - the class itself

They receive: - cls

instead of: - self

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Player:

    total_players = 0

    @classmethod
    def show_total(cls):
        print(cls.total_players)

docs.title: 🔗 cls
docs.text:
cls refers to: - the class itself

Similar to how: - self refers to the object

docs.title: Using the Class Method
docs.text: Player.show_total()

docs.title: Why Class Methods Are Useful
docs.text: Useful for: - shared class logic - object counters - factory methods - alternative constructors

docs.title: 🧩 Static Methods
docs.text:
Static methods belong to the class but do NOT use: - self - cls

They behave like utility/helper functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class MathUtils:

    @staticmethod
    def add(a, b):
        return a + b

docs.title: Using the Static Method
docs.text:
print(MathUtils.add(2, 3))

Output:

5

docs.title: Why Static Methods Are Useful
docs.text: Useful for: - utility functions - calculations - helper logic - formatting functions

docs.title: 📊 Method Comparison Table
docs.text: Method Type Uses self Uses cls Common Usage Instance Method ✅ ❌ Object behavior Class Method ❌ ✅ Shared class logic Static Method ❌ ❌ Utility/helper functions

docs.title: 🏗️ Factory Methods
docs.text: Factory methods are one of the most common uses of: - @classmethod

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Drone:

    def __init__(self, battery):
        self.battery = battery

    @classmethod
    def default_drone(cls):
        return cls(100)

docs.title: Using the Factory Method
docs.text:
drone = Drone.default_drone()

This creates: - a default object configuration

docs.title: Shared Configuration
docs.text:
class GameConfig:

    TILE_SIZE = 64

docs.title: Parser Helpers
docs.text:
class ConfigParser:

    @staticmethod
    def clean_line(line):
        ...

docs.title: Object Counter
docs.text:
class Drone:

    total_drones = 0

docs.title: Factory Method
docs.text:
class Maze:

    @classmethod
    def default_maze(cls):
        ...

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting self
docs.text:
Wrong:

def jump():

Correct:

def jump(self):

docs.title: ❌ Using self in Static Methods
docs.text:
Wrong:

@staticmethod
def test():
    print(self.name)

Static methods do not have: - self

docs.title: ❌ Confusing Class and Instance Attributes
docs.text:
Wrong:

class Enemy:
    hp = []

Mutable shared attributes may create bugs.

docs.title: ❌ Using Class Methods Like Instance Methods
docs.text:
Class methods work with: - class-level data

Not: - object-specific state

docs.title: 📚 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use instance methods for object behavior
  • Use static methods for utilities
  • Use class methods for shared class logic
  • Keep classes focused
  • Avoid giant classes
  • Use meaningful method names

docs.title: Instance Method
docs.text: Use when: - logic depends on object data

docs.title: Class Method
docs.text: Use when: - logic depends on class-wide data

docs.title: Static Method
docs.text: Use when: - no object/class state is required

docs.title: 📚 Final Notes
docs.text:
Understanding methods and attributes is one of the most important parts of Python OOP.

These concepts help: - organize projects - improve readability - structure large applications - reduce duplicated logic

They become especially useful in: - MLX projects - parsers - APIs - games - collaborative repositories

docs.title: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) Concepts
docs.text:
A beginner-friendly guide to the main Object-Oriented Programming concepts used in Python.

This document explains: - Classes - Objects - Inheritance - Encapsulation - Polymorphism - Abstraction - Composition vs Inheritance

These concepts are heavily used in: - game development - APIs - large Python applications - design patterns - 42 Python modules

docs.title: What is OOP?
docs.text:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming style based on objects.

Objects: - store data - contain behavior - interact with each other

OOP helps organize large projects into reusable and maintainable structures.

docs.title: Classes
docs.text: A class is a blueprint for creating objects.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Duck:

    def quack(self) -> None:
        print("Quack!")

docs.title: Creating an Object
docs.text:
duck = Duck()

duck.quack()

Output:

Quack!

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text: Attributes store data inside objects.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Player:

    def __init__(self, name: str) -> None:
        self.name = name

docs.title: init()
docs.text:
__init__() is called automatically when an object is created.

It initializes the object attributes.

docs.title: Creating the Object
docs.text: player = Player("Sara")

docs.title: Accessing Attributes
docs.text:
print(player.name)

Output:

Sara

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Methods are functions inside classes.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Enemy:

    def attack(self) -> None:
        print("Enemy attacks!")

docs.title: Inheritance
docs.text:
Inheritance allows one class to reuse another class.

A child class inherits: - attributes - methods - behavior

from a parent class.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Animal:

    def speak(self) -> None:
        print("Some sound")


class Dog(Animal):

    def bark(self) -> None:
        print("Woof!")

docs.title: Using Inheritance
docs.text:
dog = Dog()

dog.speak()
dog.bark()

Output:

Some sound
Woof!

docs.title: Why Use Inheritance?
docs.text: Inheritance: - reduces duplicated code - improves organization - allows shared behavior

docs.title: Overriding Methods
docs.text: Child classes can replace parent behavior.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Animal:

    def speak(self) -> None:
        print("Animal sound")


class Cat(Animal):

    def speak(self) -> None:
        print("Meow")

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
cat = Cat()

cat.speak()

Output:

Meow

This is called method overriding.

docs.title: Polymorphism
docs.text: Polymorphism means: - different objects - same method name - different behavior

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Dog:

    def speak(self) -> None:
        print("Woof")


class Cat:

    def speak(self) -> None:
        print("Meow")

docs.title: Using Polymorphism
docs.text:
animals = [Dog(), Cat()]

for animal in animals:
    animal.speak()

Output:

Woof
Meow

Same method: - different implementations

docs.title: Encapsulation
docs.text:
Encapsulation means protecting internal object data.

Objects should control access to their own state.

docs.title: Protected Attributes
docs.text:
Convention: - _name

Means: - "internal use" - should not be modified directly

docs.title: Private Attributes
docs.text:
Convention: - __name

Makes direct access harder.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class BankAccount:

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.__balance = 0

docs.title: Access Problem
docs.text:
account = BankAccount()

print(account.__balance)

This raises an error.

docs.title: Controlled Access
docs.text: Use methods instead.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class BankAccount:

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.__balance = 0

    def deposit(self, amount: int) -> None:
        self.__balance += amount

    def get_balance(self) -> int:
        return self.__balance

docs.title: Abstraction
docs.text:
Abstraction hides implementation details.

The user interacts with: - simple interfaces - not internal complexity

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class CoffeeMachine:

    def make_coffee(self) -> None:
        print("Making coffee...")

The user: - presses a button - does not care about internal machine logic

docs.title: Abstract Classes
docs.text: Python supports abstract classes using ABC.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod


class Creature(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def attack(self) -> None:
        pass

docs.title: Child Implementation
docs.text:
class Dragon(Creature):

    def attack(self) -> None:
        print("Fire breath!")

docs.title: Why Use Abstraction?
docs.text:
Abstraction: - forces structure - improves consistency - defines required behavior

Very useful in: - game systems - factories - APIs - strategy patterns

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Composition means: - objects contain other objects

Instead of inheriting behavior.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Engine:

    def start(self) -> None:
        print("Engine started")


class Car:

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.engine = Engine()

docs.title: Using Composition
docs.text:
car = Car()

car.engine.start()

docs.title: Inheritance
docs.text:
Represents: - "is-a" relationship

Example:

Dog is an Animal

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Represents: - "has-a" relationship

Example:

Car has an Engine

docs.title: Which is Better?
docs.text:
Composition is often safer and more flexible.

Inheritance is useful when: - behavior is truly shared

Composition is useful when: - objects collaborate together

docs.title: Inheritance
docs.text: class Enemy(GameObject):

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
class Game:
    self.player = Player()

docs.title: Polymorphism
docs.text:
enemy.attack()
boss.attack()

Different enemies: - same method - different behavior

docs.title: Common OOP Benefits
docs.text:
• Better code organization
  • Reusability
  • Easier maintenance
  • Cleaner architecture
  • Scalability for large projects

docs.title: Common OOP Problems
docs.text: Bad OOP design may cause: - overly deep inheritance trees - tightly coupled systems - hard debugging - unnecessary complexity

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Prefer composition over excessive inheritance
  • Keep classes focused on one responsibility
  • Use meaningful class names
  • Avoid giant classes
  • Encapsulate sensitive data
  • Use abstraction for shared interfaces

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Concept Meaning Class Blueprint for objects Object Instance of a class Inheritance Reuse behavior from another class Encapsulation Protect internal data Polymorphism Same method, different behavior Abstraction Hide implementation details Composition Objects containing objects

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
OOP is one of the most important programming paradigms in Python.

Understanding these concepts helps when building: - games - MLX projects - APIs - parsers - large applications - design pattern systems

Strong OOP foundations make code: - cleaner - safer - easier to scale

docs.title: mypy and flake8 Guide
docs.text:
A beginner-friendly guide to static analysis tools commonly used in Python projects.

This document explains: - mypy - flake8 - line too long - unused imports - indentation problems - naming conventions - common errors in 42 projects

These tools help improve: - code quality - readability - maintainability - type safety

docs.title: What is mypy?
docs.text:
mypy is a static type checker for Python.

It checks: - type hints - return types - Optional issues - invalid assignments - typing consistency

mypy analyzes code WITHOUT running it.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b

Correct typing: - parameters are integers - return value is integer

docs.title: Example Error
docs.text:
age: int = "hello"

mypy error:

Incompatible types in assignment

docs.title: Running mypy
docs.text: mypy .

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def get_name() -> str:
    print("Sara")

Problem: - function says it returns str - but returns nothing

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
def get_name() -> str:
    return "Sara"

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import Optional

name: Optional[str] = None

print(name.upper())

Possible problem: - name may be None

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
if name is not None:
    print(name.upper())

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
numbers: list[int] = ["hello"]

mypy error: - expected integer - received string

docs.title: What is flake8?
docs.text:
flake8 is a style and linting tool.

It checks: - formatting - style problems - unused imports - indentation - naming conventions

flake8 improves readability and consistency.

docs.title: Running flake8
docs.text: flake8 .

docs.title: line too long
docs.text: One of the most common flake8 errors.

docs.title: Example Error
docs.text: E501 line too long

docs.title: Problem Example
docs.text: print("This is an extremely long line that exceeds the maximum allowed line length in flake8")

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Long lines: - reduce readability - are harder to review - look bad in terminals

docs.title: Fix
docs.text: Split the line.

docs.title: Example Fix
docs.text:
print(
    "This is an extremely long line "
    "that was split correctly"
)

docs.title: unused imports
docs.text: Very common error.

docs.title: Example Error
docs.text: F401 'os' imported but unused

docs.title: Problem Example
docs.text:
import os

If os is never used: - flake8 complains

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Remove the import.

# removed unused import

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Unused imports: - create clutter - confuse developers - slow large projects slightly

docs.title: indentation
docs.text:
Python depends on indentation.

Bad indentation causes: - syntax errors - readability problems

docs.title: Example Problem
docs.text:
def hello():
print("Hello")

docs.title: Correct Version
docs.text:
def hello():
    print("Hello")

docs.title: Recommended Indentation
docs.text:
PEP8 recommends: - 4 spaces

Avoid: - tabs mixed with spaces

docs.title: naming conventions
docs.text: Python follows naming standards called: - PEP8 naming conventions

docs.title: Variables and Functions
docs.text:
Use:

snake_case

docs.title: Good Example
docs.text:
player_name = "Sara"

def calculate_score():
    pass

docs.title: Bad Example
docs.text:
PlayerName = "Sara"

def CalculateScore():
    pass

docs.title: Classes
docs.text:
Use:

PascalCase

docs.title: Good Example
docs.text:
class SpaceStation:
    pass

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
Use:

UPPER_CASE

docs.title: Example
docs.text: WINDOW_WIDTH = 1920

docs.title: Why Naming Matters
docs.text: Good naming: - improves readability - explains intent - helps teamwork - simplifies debugging

docs.title: Common 42 flake8 Problems
docs.text: Error Meaning E501 line too long F401 unused import E302 expected blank lines E305 expected blank line after function W293 blank line contains whitespace

docs.title: Example: Blank Line Error
docs.text:
Problem:

def hello():
    print("Hi")
def bye():
    print("Bye")

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
def hello():
    print("Hi")


def bye():
    print("Bye")

docs.title: Strict mypy Mode
docs.text:
Many projects use:

mypy . --strict

This enables: - stricter checks - stronger typing rules

docs.title: Common Strict Errors
docs.text:
• missing annotations
  • missing return types
  • Any usage
  • Optional problems

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

Strict mode complains because: - parameters are untyped

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b

docs.title: Good Workflow
docs.text:
Run both tools regularly:

flake8 .
mypy .

docs.title: flake8
docs.text: Focuses on: - style - formatting - readability

docs.title: mypy
docs.text: Focuses on: - typing - logic consistency - type safety

docs.title: Recommended 42 Workflow
docs.text:
1. Write code
  2. Run program
  3. Run flake8
  4. Run mypy
  5. Fix warnings immediately

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep lines short
  • Remove unused imports
  • Use meaningful names
  • Type all functions
  • Avoid unnecessary Any
  • Keep formatting consistent
  • Run tools frequently

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Tool Purpose mypy Static type checking flake8 Style and linting E501 Line too long F401 Unused import snake_case Variable/function naming PascalCase Class naming

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
mypy and flake8 are extremely important in professional Python development.

These tools help create: - cleaner code - safer projects - better teamwork - easier debugging - more maintainable applications

They are especially valuable in: - 42 projects - large Python applications - APIs - parsers - game engines - backend systems

docs.title: Python Typing Guide
docs.text:
A beginner-friendly guide to Python type hints and typing concepts commonly used in modern Python projects.

This document focuses on the typing features frequently used in 42 Python modules and real-world applications.

docs.title: Why Use Typing?
docs.text:
Type hints improve:

  • Code readability
  • Error detection
  • IDE autocompletion
  • Project maintainability
  • Static analysis with mypy

Typing makes code easier to understand and safer to maintain.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
name: str = "Sara"
age: int = 20
height: float = 1.70
is_active: bool = True

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• a: int → parameter must be an integer
  • -> int → function returns an integer

docs.title: list[str]
docs.text: Used for lists containing only strings.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: names: list[str] = ["Sara", "John", "Alice"]

docs.title: dict[str, int]
docs.text: Dictionary where: - keys are strings - values are integers

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
student_scores: dict[str, int] = {
    "Sara": 18,
    "John": 15
}

docs.title: tuple[int, int]
docs.text:
Tuple with two integers.

Very common in: - game development - coordinates - maze projects - pathfinding systems

docs.title: Example
docs.text: position: tuple[int, int] = (5, 10)

docs.title: Optional
docs.text: Optional means a value can be: - a specific type - or None

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import Optional

nickname: Optional[str] = None

Equivalent to:

nickname: str | None = None

docs.title: Union
docs.text: Union allows multiple possible types.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import Union

value: Union[int, str]

Equivalent to:

value: int | str

docs.title: Any
docs.text: Any disables type checking for a variable.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import Any

data: Any

docs.title: Callable
docs.text: Callable describes functions as types.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from collections.abc import Callable

operation: Callable[[int, int], int]

Meaning: - accepts two integers - returns one integer

docs.title: Callable Function Example
docs.text:
from collections.abc import Callable

def calculate(
    a: int,
    b: int,
    operation: Callable[[int, int], int]
) -> int:

    return operation(a, b)

docs.title: Nested Typing
docs.text: Typing structures can be combined.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
maze: list[list[int]]

A list containing lists of integers.

docs.title: Complex Example
docs.text:
player_positions: dict[str, tuple[int, int]]

Dictionary: - key → player name - value → (x, y) coordinates

docs.title: Maze coordinates
docs.text: position: tuple[int, int]

docs.title: BFS queue
docs.text: queue: list[tuple[int, int]]

docs.title: Graph structure
docs.text: graph: dict[str, list[str]]

docs.title: Type Aliases
docs.text: Useful for improving readability.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Position = tuple[int, int]

player_position: Position
enemy_position: Position

docs.title: Static Type Checking with mypy
docs.text: mypy checks typing without running the code.

docs.title: Run mypy
docs.text: mypy .

docs.title: Missing return statement
docs.text:
def get_value() -> int:
    pass

docs.title: Incompatible types
docs.text: age: int = "hello"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Avoid unnecessary Any
  • Use precise types
  • Use Optional when needed
  • Keep function return types explicit
  • Prefer readable typing over overly complex typing

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Typing Feature Purpose list[str] List of strings dict[str, int] Dictionary with string keys and integer values tuple[int, int] Tuple with two integers Optional[T] Type or None Union[A, B] Multiple possible types Any Disables type checking Callable Function as a type

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
Typing is not only about satisfying mypy.

Good typing: - documents code - improves maintainability - helps teamwork - reduces bugs - makes large projects easier to manage
